On every render, the app looks at step and conditionally displays only the relevant components for that step while leaving the server-side booking state intact.

Context between booking.html and other pages is maintained with URL parameters. When navigating to promotions.html from the promo section, the app includes a query parameter (such as highlight) so that promotions.html can emphasize local move promotions. When navigating to insurance.html from the insurance section, the app sets source=booking and suggested_coverage to a value derived from the current booking (for example, the current insured_value) so that insurance.html can tailor its content.

The page never uses localStorage or other client-side persistent storage for business-critical state. All move configuration and pricing state lives in the server-managed MoveBooking and related entities, accessed and mutated only through the assigned interfaces. URL parameters are used only to initialize context, select steps, and coordinate navigation with other pages.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters on load, determines source_type and source_reference_id, calls startMoveBooking to initialize or resume a draft MoveBooking, applies any direct presets (service_type, home_size, move_date, package_id, insurance_plan_id, insured_value), loads form options via getMoveBookingFormOptions, and retrieves the initial summary via getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: parse URL parameters and call startMoveBooking(source_type, source_reference_id).
onPageLoad: after booking is initialized, call updateCurrentMoveBooking with any preset service_type, home_size, move_date, or package_id if they differ from the booking.
onPageLoad: if insurance_plan_id or insured_value is present in URL, call updateCurrentBookingInsurance(insurance_plan_id, insured_value).
onPageLoad: call getMoveBookingFormOptions to populate selection options for subsequent components.
onPageLoad: call getCurrentMoveBookingSummary to render the initial summary state.

components.functionality: Controls which booking step is active based on the step query parameter and allows navigation between steps while ensuring the current MoveBooking has all required data before advancing.
event_handlers: onInit: read step from URL; default to the first step when absent.
onClickStep: update the step query parameter in the URL to the clicked step, optionally preventing navigation if required fields for prior steps are missing.
onNext: validate the current step’s required MoveBooking fields; if valid, change step in the URL to the next step.
onBack: change the step query parameter in the URL to the previous step without altering booking data.

components.functionality: Displays and edits core service configuration including service_type, property_type, home_size, crew_size, includes_truck, and optional labor_service_option based on getMoveBookingFormOptions and the current MoveBooking.
event_handlers: onInit: populate dropdowns for service_type, property_type, home_size, crew_size using data from getMoveBookingFormOptions.
onServiceTypeChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with the selected service_type and clear or adjust fields incompatible with the new type; then refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onPropertyTypeChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with the selected property_type.
onHomeSizeChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with the selected home_size and refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onCrewSizeChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with the selected crew_size and refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onIncludesTruckToggle: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with includes_truck set to the new value and refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onLaborServiceOptionChange: for labor-only bookings, call updateCurrentMoveBooking with labor_service_option_id.
onContinue: validate that required fields (such as service_type and home_size) are present in MoveBooking; if valid, advance the step query parameter to the address step via the stepper.

components.functionality: Collects origin and destination addresses for move bookings or a single job location for labor-only bookings, syncing changes to the current MoveBooking.
event_handlers: onInit: prefill address fields with origin_*, destination_*, or job_location_* from the current MoveBooking if available.
onOriginAddressChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with updated origin_street, origin_city, and origin_zip.
onDestinationAddressChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with updated destination_street, destination_city, and destination_zip.
onJobLocationChange: for labor-only bookings, call updateCurrentMoveBooking with updated job_location_street, job_location_city, and job_location_zip.
onContinue: validate that required address fields are present for the selected service_type; if valid, update the step query parameter to the package selection step.

components.functionality: Lists available MovePackage options for the current booking’s service_type and move_scope using getAvailableMovePackages, allows the user to filter by budget or rate, select a package, and toggle add-ons, and updates the booking accordingly.
event_handlers: onStepEnter: call getAvailableMovePackages with filters derived from MoveBooking (service_type, move_scope, crew_size, includes_truck, and any user-specified rate constraints).
onPackageFilterChange: re-call getAvailableMovePackages or refilter the existing package list client-side and re-render available options.

onPackageSelect: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with package_id set to the selected MovePackage.id and, if appropriate, align crew_size and includes_truck in the booking with the package values; then refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onAddOnToggle: when an add-on is toggled, update the add_on_ids array via updateCurrentMoveBooking and refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onEstimatedHoursChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with estimated_hours set to the user’s input and refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.

onContinue: ensure that either a package_id is selected or required configuration fields (such as crew_size and includes_truck) are set; if valid, advance the URL step parameter to the time selection step.

components.functionality: Allows the user to pick a move date and choose from available MoveTimeSlot options for that date and configuration using getAvailableTimeSlotsForCurrentBooking, then persists the selected timeslot_id.
event_handlers: onStepEnter: initialize the date picker with move_date from MoveBooking or any move_date URL parameter.

onDateChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with the new move_date; upon success, call getAvailableTimeSlotsForCurrentBooking with move_date and optional user-entered max_hourly_rate or max_total_estimate.
onTimeslotFilterChange: re-call getAvailableTimeSlotsForCurrentBooking with updated sorting or max rate filters, then re-render the slots.
onTimeslotSelect: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with timeslot_id set to the selected MoveTimeSlot.id and refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onContinue: validate that MoveBooking has a timeslot_id; if valid, update the step query parameter to the insurance step.

components.functionality: Displays available InsurancePlan options from getMoveBookingFormOptions and the current booking’s insurance selection, allows the user to pick a plan and adjust insured_value, and persists these choices via updateCurrentBookingInsurance.
event_handlers: onStepEnter: use insurance_plan_options from getMoveBookingFormOptions and the booking’s insurance_plan_id/insured_value to initialize the selection UI.
onInsurancePlanChange: call updateCurrentBookingInsurance with the selected insurance_plan_id and the current insured_value; then refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onInsuredValueChange: call updateCurrentBookingInsurance with the current insurance_plan_id and updated insured_value; then refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary.
onLearnMoreClick: navigate to insurance.html with source=booking and suggested_coverage set from the current booking’s insured_value or an appropriate derived value.
onContinue: advance the step query parameter to the summary step without additional validation if insurance is optional, or after confirming an insurance decision if required.

components.functionality: Allows the user to enter, apply, and manage a promo code for the current MoveBooking using applyPromoCodeToCurrentBooking, updating discounts and totals in the summary.
event_handlers: onApplyPromo: call applyPromoCodeToCurrentBooking with the user-entered promo_code; if success is true, update the UI using the returned booking and applied_promotion and refresh getCurrentMoveBookingSummary; otherwise display the message as an error.

onRemovePromo: optionally call applyPromoCodeToCurrentBooking with an empty or cleared promo_code if supported, or trigger a booking update sequence to clear promo-related fields (handled server-side), then refresh the summary.
onViewDealsClick: navigate to promotions.html with query parameters indicating local move promotions should be highlighted.

components.functionality: Shows a real-time overview of the booking details and pricing using getCurrentMoveBookingSummary, and is used on multiple steps (packages, time, insurance, summary) to provide immediate feedback on changes.
event_handlers: onInit: call getCurrentMoveBookingSummary to populate initial summary fields.

onBookingUpdated: whenever an updateCurrentMoveBooking, updateCurrentBookingInsurance, applyPromoCodeToCurrentBooking, or confirmCurrentMoveBooking call succeeds, re-call getCurrentMoveBookingSummary and re-render the summary.
onExpandSectionToggle: allow the user to expand or collapse sections (such as cost breakdown, addresses, or add-ons) without changing server state.

components.functionality: Collects and edits the customer’s contact_name, contact_phone, and contact_email for the MoveBooking and coordinates final confirmation via confirmCurrentMoveBooking.
event_handlers: onInit: prefill contact fields from MoveBooking if the booking originated from a QuoteRequest or elsewhere with existing contact information.
onContactFieldChange: call updateCurrentMoveBooking with the changed contact_name, contact_phone, or contact_email so the booking draft remains up to date.

onConfirmClick: validate that contact_name, contact_phone, and contact_email are present and that the terms acceptance control is checked; then call confirmCurrentMoveBooking with the final contact fields and accept_terms set accordingly.
onConfirmSuccess: display confirmation_number and updated booking_status from the returned booking, and optionally disable further edits.
onConfirmError: show the message returned from confirmCurrentMoveBooking and keep the form active for correction.

components.functionality: Displays the outcome of the final booking confirmation, including the confirmation number and any final instructions, based on the result of confirmCurrentMoveBooking.
event_handlers: onConfirmationResult: when confirmCurrentMoveBooking returns, update the panel with the booking’s booking_status and confirmation_number.
onViewBookingDetailsClick: keep the user on the page but highlight the summary panel with the finalized MoveBooking data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (likely around 1140–1200px) and large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom flow mirroring the mockup. At the very top, a full-width hero band with a darkened photographic background introduces the Move Booking experience. Centered within this hero is the booking initializer call-to-action. Below the hero, the background switches to solid white, and a prominent, wide, rounded orange booking card sits at the center of the page. Inside this card, content is organized in a two-column layout on larger screens (main step content on the left, summary on the right) but collapses into a single column on smaller viewports. The stepper navigation anchors the top of the orange card, and all form/selection components appear in a linear sequence underneath, driven by the active step.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom flow mirroring the mockup. At the very top, a full-width hero band with a darkened photographic background introduces the Move Booking experience. Centered within this hero is the booking initializer call-to-action. Below the hero, the background switches to solid white, and a prominent, wide, rounded orange booking card sits at the center of the page. Inside this card, content is organized in a two-column layout on larger screens (main step content on the left, summary on the right) but collapses into a single column on smaller viewports. The stepper navigation anchors the top of the orange card, and all form/selection components appear in a linear sequence underneath, driven by the active step.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 45–55% of the initial viewport height is used by the hero section (dark image, headline, initializer). Below this, the main booking section occupies the remainder of the page with generous vertical padding (64–96px) above the section heading and around the orange booking card. The orange card itself spans about 80–88% of the content width within a centered 12-column grid, with the inner left column (forms/step content) using approximately 7–8 columns and the right column (summary) using 4–5 columns on desktop. On mobile, both stack into a single column that spans nearly the full content width, with consistent 16–24px internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps density low-to-medium. Each major area (hero content, stepper, individual step forms, summary panel) sits in its own visually separated block with generous white space around and between them. Form fields are grouped in logical clusters with breathing room: 16–24px vertical spacing between field groups, 24–32px padding inside cards and panels, and at least 32–48px separation between the stepper and the first form in each step. No area feels crowded; the design favors clarity, short text labels, and visible hierarchy, guided by the spacing scale.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the top-left logo in the floating nav (outside this spec) and quickly moves to the central hero heading and the vibrant initializer control, encouraging them to begin the booking. Scrolling or activating the initializer moves attention to the section heading, then immediately to the large orange booking card. Within this card, the primary flow runs from top to bottom: stepper navigation at the top, then the active step’s main input area on the left. The right-hand booking summary acts as a persistent secondary focal point, updating as the user progresses. The flow across steps moves logically from service details and addresses, to package and time selection, to add-ons, promos, contact details, and finally to the confirmation panel, which becomes the new primary focal area when the process completes.

layout.overall_description: The Move Booking page opens with a full-width hero section over a dark, desaturated photo related to moving (e.g., a truck or boxes), subtly blurred and overlaid with a dark tint. Centered in this hero, above the fold, is a concise heading about booking your move, a short supporting line, and the booking initializer control—all vertically stacked with generous spacing, aligned centrally to echo the friendly corporate style. Below this hero, the background shifts sharply to white, and a centered heading introduces the booking flow with ample space above and below. Dominating the mid-screen is a large, wide, pill-cornered orange card that feels like a floating panel, spanning most of the content width but leaving comfortable white gutters on either side. At the top of this card sits a horizontal stepper navigation, guiding the user through the multi-step booking process. Beneath the stepper, the card is split into a main left column for each step’s interactive content (forms and selectors) and a slightly narrower right column containing a summary panel with a light elevation and white interior. As the user moves step-by-step, the left-hand content area changes while the right-hand summary remains in view, giving a sense of continuity and control. Once the booking is confirmed, the left side of the orange card transitions into a confirmation status panel that takes visual priority, while the summary on the right reinforces the finalized details. The entire page feels spacious, centralized, and visually coherent, with orange and green accents layered over a quiet dark-photo hero and clean white body background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking initializer sits within the hero section, centrally aligned horizontally and vertically in the lower half of the hero content stack. Directly below the main hero heading and short supporting text, it appears as a wide, rounded button or compact panel, spanning roughly 40–55% of the content width on desktop, with generous 16–24px padding and strong orange coloring. It is surrounded by clear whitespace: about 24–32px above (to the subheading) and 32–48px below (to the bottom of the hero), reinforcing it as the next action. On mobile, it expands to nearly full width with preserved vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page, acting as the transition cue into the booking section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking stepper navigation is positioned at the very top inside the large centered orange booking card, just below the section heading and above any forms. It runs horizontally across nearly the full width of the orange card (spanning about 90–95% of its inner width), with equal-width step indicators arranged from left to right along the 12-column grid. There is a 24–32px top padding between the top edge of the card and the stepper, and about 24px of space below the stepper before the first form element appears. On mobile, the stepper condenses into either a horizontally scrollable or icon-and-label row but remains anchored to the top of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the orange card on larger screens, remaining pinned to the top edge of the card as the user scrolls through tall form sections, while the card itself scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service details form occupies the upper portion of the left-hand column inside the orange booking card during the first step. It is positioned directly beneath the stepper navigation, aligned to the left edge of the inner content area, and spans about 7–8 of the 12 grid columns (roughly 60–65% of the card width) on desktop. The form is grouped into a light, slightly elevated white or very light tint sub-card with 24px padding, leaving at least 24–32px of orange background margin on all sides within the main card. Vertically, it uses 16–24px spacing between individual field rows, with larger 24px separations between logical groups (e.g., move type, size, and special requirements). On mobile, this form stretches nearly full width, with consistent padding and vertical stacking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it is the first block of interactive content and scrolls naturally underneath the sticky stepper.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The address form is placed immediately below the service details form within the same left-hand column in the first step, creating a sense of a single, continuous 'Step 1' area. It aligns to the same left and right boundaries as the service details form, maintaining the same 7–8 column width and inner padding. There is a clear 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the service details form and the top of the address form, both visually framed by the orange background of the main card. The address form itself can be structured into two stacked sub-sections (origin and destination) with a thin horizontal separation and 16–24px spacing between address fields. On smaller screens, the address form appears directly below the service details form, maintaining the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing directly after the service details form in the scroll sequence for the first step.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package selector replaces the service and address forms in the left-hand column when the user advances to the package selection step. It is centered within the same left content width, spanning about 7–8 grid columns, and begins directly under the stepper with 24–32px of top spacing. Inside this area, individual package options are displayed as a horizontal or two-column grid of large, rounded cards, each about 40–48% of the left column width, with 16–24px gaps between them. The cards sit on the orange background with their own white fill, creating a clear, tappable layout. On mobile, these package cards stack vertically, each stretching nearly full width with comfortable vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it becomes the sole active content block for its step, scrolling underneath the sticky stepper while the summary remains visible to the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot selector is placed in the same left-hand content zone as other steps, appearing directly beneath the stepper when the user reaches the scheduling step. It spans the same 7–8 column width and is structured as a vertically stacked combination of a compact date selector (e.g., a small calendar or date pills) at the top and a grid or list of available time slots below. The top of this block sits about 24–32px under the stepper, with 16–24px spacing between the date and time slot sections. Time slots are arranged in rows of pill-shaped buttons or small cards, filling the available width with 8–16px gaps. On mobile, the layout collapses to a single column, with the date selector occupying full width and time slot options appearing as a stacked list or two-up grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the main left column for its step; scrolls under the sticky stepper and alongside the right-hand summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The insurance selector appears in the left column during the insurance step, starting just below the stepper with the same 24–32px top spacing. It uses the full 7–8 column width and is composed of a vertical stack of 2–3 option cards or radio-row blocks, each with a short title, key coverage details, and price. Each option card is full-width within the left column, with 16–24px vertical gaps between them and 16–24px internal padding. The overall block is visually simple and compact, with plenty of whitespace above and below to maintain the low-to-medium density. On smaller screens, it remains a straightforward vertical list that the user can quickly scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, flowing under the stepper and updating the right-hand summary dynamically as options are chosen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo code form resides in the same left-hand content area as other steps and appears at the top of the final details step, above the contact details form. It is a horizontally oriented, compact stripe spanning the full left column width, with a text input on the left (about 65–70% of the width) and an 'Apply' button on the right (about 30–35%), all inside a lightly elevated, rounded sub-card. There is about 24px of vertical spacing above it (from the stepper) and 24px below it before the contact details form begins. On mobile, the input and button stack vertically with 8–16px spacing, but the block still reads as a single, self-contained band inside the orange card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it anchors the top of the final step’s content, always appearing immediately above the contact details form within the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking summary panel is positioned to the right of all step content within the orange booking card, forming a secondary but persistent column. On desktop, it occupies approximately 4–5 of the 12 grid columns (about 30–35% of the card width) and is aligned to the right inner edge of the orange card, leaving a consistent 24–32px horizontal gap from the left column content. The panel is itself a vertical white card with subtle shadow and rounded corners, inset from the orange card edges by 16–24px, and extending vertically to show key booking details, totals, and updates. There is 24px padding inside, with grouped sections separated by 16–24px. On mobile and smaller tablets, this panel moves below the main step content, spanning the full width of the orange card with preserved internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky relative to the viewport on larger screens, remaining visible at the right as the user scrolls through taller left-column content, while respecting the top and bottom padding of the orange card; on small screens, it becomes static and scrolls normally after the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact details form is placed directly below the promo code form within the left-hand column during the final step of the booking. It uses the same 7–8 column width and is framed in a white or lightly tinted sub-card with 24px internal padding, set against the orange background of the main card. There is a clear 24px vertical gap between the promo code form and the top of this form, and 24–32px of breathing room below it before the bottom of the orange card’s inner padding. The form fields (name, email, phone, etc.) are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing, reinforcing clarity and ease of completion. On smaller screens, it simply extends as a full-width block below the promo section, maintaining the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it forms the final primary input block before the user submits and moves to confirmation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Once the booking is submitted, the confirmation status panel takes over the left-hand area of the orange booking card and becomes the main focal element. It starts directly beneath the stepper (or replaces it with a simpler progress indicator) and spans the full left-column width (7–8 columns). The panel itself is a prominent white card with rounded corners, centered vertically within the visible portion of the orange card when first shown, and padded with 24–32px on all sides. It displays a large confirmation icon and headline at the top, with booking reference details and next steps in smaller text below, separated by 16–24px spacing. The right-hand booking summary panel remains visible, now reinforcing the finalized details alongside this confirmation. On mobile, the confirmation panel appears above the summary panel in the single-column stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the orange card but replaces the previous step content in the left column; it may initially be scrolled into view automatically after submission, then behaves like a normal card as the user scrolls.

name: Pricing & Packages
title: Pricing & Packages
description: Browse, filter, sort, compare, and start booking available moving service packages.

core_features: Load dynamic filter and sort options for service type, move scope, hourly rate, rating, and review count using getPricingPageFilterOptions.
Display a list of available move packages based on current filters and sorting using getAvailableMovePackages.
Allow users to adjust filters (service type, move scope, maximum hourly rate, minimum rating, minimum review count) and sorting (e.g., by rating or price), with state reflected in URL parameters.
Enable users to add one or more packages to a comparison list via addPackageToComparisonList.
Show a live comparison summary (e.g., how many packages are selected) using getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary and expose navigation to compare.html when at least one package is in the list.
Allow users to initiate a booking flow for a selected package using startMoveBookingWithPackage and navigate to booking.html with the selected package ID.
user_workflows:
Initial load with optional presets:
1. User opens pricing.html directly or via header/footer navigation or another page.
2. Page reads URL parameters (service_type, move_scope, hourly_rate_max, rating_min, min_reviews, sort_by) if present.
3. Page calls getPricingPageFilterOptions to retrieve available filters (service types, move scopes, hourly rate range, rating range, review count buckets, sort options).
4. Filter controls are initialized using URL parameters when present; otherwise defaults are derived from the values returned by getPricingPageFilterOptions.
5. Page calls getAvailableMovePackages with current filter and sort state mapped from URL and control values.
6. Page calls getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary to display the current comparison count.
7. Package cards are rendered with actions for comparison and booking.

Refining results with filters and sorting:
1. User changes one or more filter controls (e.g., selects a different service type, adjusts maximum hourly rate slider, selects a minimum rating or review count, or changes move scope).
2. On each change, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (e.g., service_type, move_scope, hourly_rate_max, rating_min, min_reviews) and sort_by if applicable.
3. After the URL is updated, the page re-calls getAvailableMovePackages with the new parameters.
4. The package list refreshes to reflect the updated filters and sorting.
5. The user can share or bookmark the URL; revisiting it will re-apply the same filters and sort via the initial load workflow.

Adding packages to comparison and viewing comparison:
1. In the package list, user clicks an "Add to comparison" (or similar) control on a package card.
2. The page calls addPackageToComparisonList with that package's id.
3. On success, the page calls getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary to get the updated PackageComparisonList.
4. The comparison summary component updates to show the current number of selected packages and activates the "View comparison" navigation control.
5. User clicks the "View comparison" link or button in the summary bar.
6. The browser navigates to compare.html (no additional URL parameters required); compare.html will use the current comparison list from its own interfaces.

Starting a booking from a package:
1. User reviews package details in a package card.
2. User clicks a "Book Now" or "Select package" button on that card.
3. The page calls startMoveBookingWithPackage with the package id.
4. If the response indicates success, the page navigates to booking.html with URL parameters source=pricing and package_id set to the selected package's id.
5. The booking page uses these parameters and server-side booking state (from startMoveBookingWithPackage) to continue the booking flow.

Changing sort order without altering filters:
1. User interacts with a sort control (e.g., dropdown) to choose a new sort option.
2. The chosen sort option’s value is written to the sort_by URL parameter.
3. The page re-calls getAvailableMovePackages with the same filter parameters but updated sort_by.
4. The packages list re-renders in the new order while filters remain unchanged.
interactions:
Page load / URL change:
- Trigger: Initial navigation to pricing.html or browser navigation that changes URL query parameters.
- Behavior: Read current URL parameters; call getPricingPageFilterOptions; initialize filter and sort controls; then call getAvailableMovePackages and getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary; render components with returned data.

Filter control changes:
- Trigger: User changes service_type, move_scope, maximum hourly rate slider, minimum rating, or minimum review count controls.
- Behavior: Update corresponding URL parameters; re-invoke getAvailableMovePackages with updated parameters; update package list results without hardcoded values; keep filter controls synchronized with URL.

Sort option change:
- Trigger: User selects a different sort option.
- Behavior: Update sort_by URL parameter; re-call getAvailableMovePackages with updated sort_by; re-render package list in new order.

Add to comparison:
- Trigger: User clicks "Add to comparison" on a package card.
- Behavior: Call addPackageToComparisonList with the package id; on success, call getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary; update comparison summary component state (e.g., count, enabled/disabled state of "View comparison"); provide feedback on duplicate or failed additions using the returned message when appropriate.

View comparison:
- Trigger: User clicks "View comparison" in the comparison summary bar when at least one package exists in the comparison list.
- Behavior: Navigate to compare.html without additional query parameters; no new interface calls on this page are required at the moment of navigation.

Start booking from package:
- Trigger: User clicks "Book Now" or "Select package" on a package card.
- Behavior: Call startMoveBookingWithPackage with the selected package id; if the response indicates success, navigate to booking.html with URL parameters source=pricing and package_id set to the same package id; optionally block navigation if success=false and use the returned message to inform the user.

Pagination or result limit (if implemented):
- Trigger: User interacts with a result limit or next/previous controls (if the page applies any limit via the limit parameter of getAvailableMovePackages).
- Behavior: Update the relevant URL parameter(s) to reflect the current page or limit; re-call getAvailableMovePackages with updated parameters; re-render the list.
page_functionality.state_logic:
Page state is derived from and synchronized with URL query parameters combined with the values returned by getPricingPageFilterOptions. On load, the page reads URL parameters:
- service_type: If present, used both to initialize the service type filter control and passed as service_type to getAvailableMovePackages. If absent, the filter control defaults to a neutral or default value derived from getPricingPageFilterOptions.service_type_options.
- move_scope: If present, used to initialize the move scope filter control and passed as move_scope to getAvailableMovePackages. If absent, it defaults from getPricingPageFilterOptions.move_scope_options.
- hourly_rate_max: If present, used as the current value for the maximum hourly rate slider and passed as hourly_rate_max to getAvailableMovePackages. If absent, the slider defaults to a value derived from getPricingPageFilterOptions.hourly_rate_range (for example, the max of the available range). The slider's minimum bound is set from hourly_rate_range.min; hourly_rate_min passed to getAvailableMovePackages can be derived from the same range or left unset according to implementation but must not be hardcoded.
- rating_min: If present, used to initialize the minimum rating filter and passed to getAvailableMovePackages as rating_min. If absent, defaults are based on getPricingPageFilterOptions.rating_range.
- min_reviews: If present, used to select the corresponding bucket in getPricingPageFilterOptions.review_count_buckets, and passed to getAvailableMovePackages as min_review_count. If absent, the default bucket (or no minimum) is selected according to the API-provided buckets.
- sort_by: If present, sets the initial sort option and is passed to getAvailableMovePackages. If absent, a default is chosen from getPricingPageFilterOptions.sort_options.

When the user changes any filter or sort control, the page immediately updates the corresponding URL parameter(s), ensuring that the visible UI state (selected filters and sort) always matches the URL. The package list is always regenerated from getAvailableMovePackages using the current URL-derived state. No filter state is persisted via localStorage or other client storage; reloading the page or sharing the URL reproduces the same state.

The comparison list state is not stored in URL parameters; it is managed via backend or session state and accessed with getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary and addPackageToComparisonList. The page uses the presence and size of comparison_list.package_ids from getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary to enable or disable the 'View comparison' navigation. Booking state is similarly managed outside the URL through startMoveBookingWithPackage; the URL only passes source=pricing and package_id to booking.html.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, synchronizes URL parameters with filters and sorting, and triggers data fetches for filters, available packages, and comparison summary.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read URL parameters (service_type, move_scope, hourly_rate_max, rating_min, min_reviews, sort_by); call getPricingPageFilterOptions; initialize internal filter state from URL or defaults from the API; then call getAvailableMovePackages with mapped parameters and call getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary.

onURLParamChange: When the browser URL query string changes (e.g., via internal navigation without full reload), re-derive filter state from URL and re-call getAvailableMovePackages and getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls to filter packages by service type, move scope, maximum hourly rate, minimum rating, and minimum review count, using options from getPricingPageFilterOptions and keeping values synchronized with URL parameters.
event_handlers: onFiltersOptionsLoaded: When getPricingPageFilterOptions returns data, populate service type, move scope, hourly rate range, rating range, and review count bucket controls. Initialize each control’s selected value based on URL parameters or API-derived defaults.

onServiceTypeChange: When the user selects a service type, update the service_type URL parameter; trigger a refresh via pricing-page-init-controller to call getAvailableMovePackages with the new service_type.
onMoveScopeChange: When the user selects a move scope, update the move_scope URL parameter; trigger a refresh of getAvailableMovePackages.

onHourlyRateMaxChange: When the user adjusts the maximum hourly rate slider or numeric input, constrain the value within the dynamic min and max from hourly_rate_range; update the hourly_rate_max URL parameter; trigger a refresh of getAvailableMovePackages with hourly_rate_max set and hourly_rate_min derived or left unset according to implementation.
onRatingMinChange: When the user adjusts the minimum rating control, validate it within rating_range and write the value to the rating_min URL parameter; refresh getAvailableMovePackages.

onMinReviewsBucketChange: When the user selects a review count bucket, map it to its min_reviews value; update the min_reviews URL parameter; refresh getAvailableMovePackages with min_review_count set to this value.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose how packages are ordered (e.g., by rating or price) based on sort_options from getPricingPageFilterOptions and keeps the choice in sync with the sort_by URL parameter.
event_handlers: onSortOptionsLoaded: After getPricingPageFilterOptions returns, populate the sort control using sort_options and set the selected option matching the current sort_by URL parameter or a default from the API.

onSortChange: When the user selects a different sort option, update the sort_by URL parameter to the option’s value and re-call getAvailableMovePackages via the controller, re-rendering the package list accordingly.

components.functionality: Displays the current list of move packages returned by getAvailableMovePackages as a set of cards, each showing package details and actions for comparison and booking.
event_handlers: onPackagesLoaded: When getAvailableMovePackages returns an array of MovePackage, render each package using the package-card component, without hardcoded fields or values beyond what exists in MovePackage.

onEmptyResult: If getAvailableMovePackages returns an empty array, display a functional message or state that no packages match the current filters, and offer a way to adjust filters (e.g., via pricing-filters-panel); the message content should not hardcode specific package names or prices.

components.functionality: Represents a single MovePackage item with summary details (e.g., name, service_type, move_scope, crew_size, includes_truck, base_hourly_rate, min_hours, rating, review_count, tags) and provides controls to add to comparison or initiate booking.
event_handlers: onAddToComparisonClick: When the user clicks the comparison action for this package, call addPackageToComparisonList with the package id; after receiving the response, if success is true, trigger pricing-comparison-summary to refresh via getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary; if success is false, optionally expose the returned message to the user without hardcoded fallback text.

onBookNowClick: When the user clicks the booking action, call startMoveBookingWithPackage with the package id; if success is true, navigate to booking.html with query parameters source=pricing and package_id equal to this package's id; if success is false, optionally show the returned message and do not navigate.

components.functionality: Shows a compact summary of the current comparison list (e.g., count of packages selected) using getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary and provides navigation to compare.html when at least one package is selected.
event_handlers: onComparisonSummaryLoad: On page load and after any successful addPackageToComparisonList call, call getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary; update the summary display based on comparison_list.package_ids (e.g., show the number of items selected).
onComparisonListChange: When the length of comparison_list.package_ids changes from zero to non-zero or vice versa, enable or disable the 'View comparison' link or button accordingly.

onViewComparisonClick: If comparison_list.package_ids contains at least one id, navigate to compare.html with no extra URL parameters; if the list is empty, ignore the click or provide functional feedback using only the current state.

components.functionality: Optionally manages result limiting or pagination through the limit parameter of getAvailableMovePackages, reflecting current page or limit state via URL parameters (if such parameters are introduced) and coordinating re-fetching of packages.
event_handlers: onLimitChange: When the user changes how many packages to view per page (if such a control is present), update the corresponding URL parameter (for example, limit) and call getAvailableMovePackages with this limit value while preserving other filter and sort parameters.
onPageNavigation: If pagination is implemented by index or offset using URL parameters, update those parameters on user navigation and re-call getAvailableMovePackages.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (likely around 1140–1200px) and large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the global pattern from the mockup: a full-width hero with a darkened photo background and centered title, overlaid under a floating white nav card. Immediately below the hero, move into a white-background content area. At the top of this white area, place the page heading (left-aligned within the central column), followed directly by a large, wide, orange rounded comparison card (pricing-comparison-summary) in the center, analogous to the contact form card in the mockup. Beneath that, split the central content area into a left filter sidebar (pricing-filters-panel) and a right main column that stacks the sort control (pricing-sort-control), the package grid (packages-list with package-card items), and finally the pagination controller (packages-pagination-controller). The pricing-page-init-controller runs behind the scenes and doesn’t take visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the global pattern from the mockup: a full-width hero with a darkened photo background and centered title, overlaid under a floating white nav card. Immediately below the hero, move into a white-background content area. At the top of this white area, place the page heading (left-aligned within the central column), followed directly by a large, wide, orange rounded comparison card (pricing-comparison-summary) in the center, analogous to the contact form card in the mockup. Beneath that, split the central content area into a left filter sidebar (pricing-filters-panel) and a right main column that stacks the sort control (pricing-sort-control), the package grid (packages-list with package-card items), and finally the pagination controller (packages-pagination-controller). The pricing-page-init-controller runs behind the scenes and doesn’t take visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a centered max-width container around 1140–1200px, with generous outer gutters (~10–12% of viewport width on large screens). The hero occupies about 45–55% of the initial viewport height with ample vertical breathing room around the hero text. The white content section below has a generous top margin (64–96px) from the hero, and the orange comparison summary card occupies most of the visible width of the content column (around 70–80%), with 24–32px internal padding. The filter sidebar takes roughly 30–35% of the central content width, while the packages column takes the remaining 65–70%. Vertical gaps between major blocks (comparison card, filter/sort band, grid, pagination) follow the 32–64px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low-to-medium density: large cards, short text blocks, and clear breathing space between elements. Inside each card (filters, packages, comparison summary), use 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Between stacked sections, preserve 48–72px of vertical space to echo the airy marketing feel of the mockup, rather than a data-heavy table layout. The package grid is visually rich but not cramped—3 columns at desktop with ample gutters, transitioning to 2 and then 1 column as screens shrink.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Mirror the mockup’s Z-pattern and top-to-bottom storytelling: the eye starts at the floating nav’s logo on the left, moves across to the primary nav CTAs on the right, then drops down to the central hero headline (“Pricing & Packages”) in bright brand orange. From there, gaze flows to the white subheading and primary hero CTA, then scrolls down to the left-aligned page heading in the white section and immediately to the large orange comparison summary card that dominates the next viewport. From this card, the eye naturally moves down into the filter vs. packages area: first touching the filters on the left and the sort control on the right, then scanning across the grid of package cards. Finally, the visual journey ends at the centered pagination controls, with an option to scroll back up or refine filters.

layout.overall_description:
The page opens with a full-width hero section showing a darkened, desaturated photograph of movers or a moving truck. Floating over the upper portion of this image is the site’s signature white navigation bar: a wide, pill-shaped card centered horizontally, leaving generous left and right margins. The company logo anchors the nav on the left, navigation links run through the center, and a standout orange phone/contact button sits on the right, forming a clear Z-pattern. In the middle of the hero, vertically and horizontally centered, the page title “Pricing & Packages” appears in bold orange type, with a short white subheading below that explains that visitors can browse, compare, and book moving services. The hero occupies roughly half of the initial viewport height, with ample negative space around the text so it feels calm and premium.

As you scroll past the hero, the background shifts to a clean white band that covers the remainder of the page. At the top of this band, within a centered max-width content container, the section heading “Compare our moving packages” is placed near the left edge of the content column, with generous top and bottom spacing. Directly beneath this heading, occupying about three-quarters of the content width and centered, sits a large orange rounded rectangle card: this is the comparison summary area, slightly elevated with a soft shadow, and it immediately draws attention as the main interactive overview. Below this orange card, the layout resolves into a two-column content area within the same central container: the left third is a white filter sidebar card, and the right two-thirds form the main listing column. At the top of the right column, a slim horizontal sort control bar sits, aligned with the filter sidebar’s top edge. Under the sort bar, a grid of rounded package cards flows down the page in 3 columns with generous gutters, each card using white surfaces with strong orange/green accents for pricing and CTAs. The grid extends down, and at the bottom of the main content column, centered under the package grid, a compact pagination control cluster appears, mirroring the rounded, button-like style used elsewhere. Throughout, large margins, rounded components, and strong orange accents against white maintain the approachable, modern corporate feel of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller is conceptually placed at the very top of the Pricing & Packages content stack, just below the global hero section and above the first visible white content band, but it does not render any visible UI. It occupies no physical space in the layout; instead, it initializes filters, sorting, and comparison state before the user sees the filter panel, comparison summary card, and packages list. In terms of document flow, it sits as a logical first child inside the main content container, ensuring that all visible components beneath it are fully initialized by the time they appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic layer; exists in the DOM order above the main pricing components but does not influence visual spacing or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters panel appears in the white content section below the hero and the large orange comparison summary card. Inside the central max-width container, it anchors the left side of a two-column layout. On desktop, it occupies roughly 30–35% of the content width (about 3–4 columns in a 12-column grid), starting about 48–64px beneath the bottom of the comparison summary card. The panel itself is a tall, white rounded rectangle card with a subtle shadow, visually separated from the white page background by elevation and a slightly darker border or outline. It is top-aligned with the sort control in the right column, and extends downward alongside the package grid. Inside the card, filters are stacked vertically with 16–24px vertical spacing, and the card has 24–32px padding on all sides. On wider screens, the left edge of the filters panel aligns with the left edge of the content column, leaving equal white gutters to the viewport edges as seen in the mockup. On tablet and mobile, this panel collapses to full-width and stacks above the sort control and package list, maintaining its card-like appearance with consistent outer margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the filters panel behaves as a sticky sidebar within the scrollable content region: once scrolled into view, its top edge pins approximately 80–96px below the viewport top (to clear the floating nav/hero area), remaining visible as the package grid scrolls. On smaller screens, it becomes a standard, non-sticky full-width card stacked above the listing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned in the main content column to the right of the filters panel, at the top of the packages area. Within the 12-column grid, it spans the full width of the right column (roughly 65–70% of the central container, about 8–9 columns). The top of the sort control aligns horizontally with the top of the filters panel, creating a clean, unified baseline. Visually, it appears as a slim horizontal bar or pill-shaped control, about 56–64px tall, with rounded corners and either a very light gray or white background to keep it subtle relative to the bold orange comparison card above. The control content (label like “Sort by” and a dropdown or segment options) is right-aligned, so the eye moves from the filter column on the left toward the sort options on the right, following a slight Z-pattern within this section. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the comparison summary card and the top of the sort control. On tablet and mobile, this control becomes full-width, sitting directly below the filters panel card with the same side margins as other content for a consistent stacked layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal block flow within the main content column; it scrolls with the page and does not stick. On smaller screens, it adapts to full-width but maintains its position immediately above the packages list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages list occupies the majority of the right-hand column beneath the sort control. It begins approximately 16–24px below the bottom edge of the sort control, filling the remaining height of the content area. Within the right column, it uses a responsive grid layout: on large desktops, package cards are arranged in three columns, each card taking roughly one-third of the column width, with 24px horizontal gutters and 24–32px vertical spacing between rows; on medium screens, it collapses to two columns, and on small screens, it becomes a single-column vertical list. The grid’s left edge aligns with the left edge of the sort control, and its right edge aligns with the right edge of the central content container, creating a clean vertical rhythm. The list visually reads as a tall, spacious grid of card tiles floating against the white background, with enough whitespace around the entire grid (32–48px bottom margin) to clearly separate it from the pagination controller below. The filters panel on the left typically ends around the same vertical region as the midpoint or lower part of this grid, so the user scrolls down primarily through the right column’s cards while the left filter card remains in view on larger screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content; it expands vertically as more packages are shown and defines the main scroll length of the page. Its width and column count respond fluidly to viewport size while remaining within the central max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each package-card sits as an individual tile within the packages-list grid, occupying a full grid cell width. Visually, the card is a large, rounded rectangle with a soft elevation shadow, echoing the friendly corporate feel of the mockup’s orange cards. The card background is primarily white to keep the grid light, but it incorporates strong brand accents: an orange or green header strip at the top (spanning the card’s width, about 56–72px tall) that contains the package name or tier label, and prominent price text in dark gray or near-black underneath. The card has 24–32px internal padding on all sides, with vertical spacing between elements following the 8–16–24px rhythm. Key details such as included services, number of movers, estimated hours, and extras are presented in a compact vertical stack with bullet-style or icon-supported rows. A primary orange CTA button (e.g., “Select Package” or “Start Booking”) is positioned at the bottom of the card, full-width or nearly full-width, with 16px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding, and a generous top margin (24–32px) separating it from the last line of details. The card’s height is consistent for packages in the same row to preserve a tidy grid, and the rounded corners match the nav bar and comparison card radius for harmony.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the packages-list grid; behaves as a responsive card whose width is dictated by the grid column, while height grows slightly based on content. On hover (desktop), it can subtly lift and intensify its shadow to reinforce interactivity without shifting the overall grid layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing-comparison-summary is the dominant focal card at the top of the white content section, directly beneath the hero. Inside the central max-width container, the section heading sits near the left edge, and this card is placed immediately below it, centered horizontally. On desktop, it spans roughly 70–80% of the content width (around 8–10 out of 12 grid columns) and is vertically separated from the hero by a generous gap of about 64–96px. The card is a large, pill-like orange rectangle with pronounced rounded corners, soft shadow, and 24–32px padding on all sides. Its height is substantial—approximately 200–260px—so it occupies a significant portion of the first scroll in the white section. Internally, information is arranged in a clear, horizontally segmented layout: for example, a left block summarizing the number of selected packages and total/average cost, a middle block suggesting the best value or recommended option, and a right-hand area with a primary CTA button like “Compare & Continue” or “Start Booking.” Text is white or very light for contrast against the orange, with a large headline or key metric near the top left of the card and supporting details beneath. The card is visually analogous to the contact form card in the original mockup: it is the main interactive focal point that sets context before the more detailed controls and lists below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default, it sits in the normal document flow between the hero and the filter/sort area. On larger screens, a compact version of this card can optionally become sticky at the top of the content area once the user scrolls past it, pinning just below the fixed nav/hero boundary to keep key comparison info and CTAs in view while scrolling through packages; when sticky, its width reduces slightly to match the main content column and its height compresses for minimal obstruction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controller is located at the bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the packages-list grid. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the last row of package cards and the top of the pagination controls, creating a clear end-of-list cue. The pagination element is centered horizontally within the content container, not just within the right column, so it visually anchors the entire section. It spans roughly 40–60% of the content width, appearing as a compact row of rounded buttons (page numbers, previous/next arrows) against the white background. The primary page button and navigation arrows use the brand orange for emphasis, while inactive pages are light gray or outlined. The control has 16–24px vertical padding, with individual buttons separated by 8–16px to maintain the clean, low-density feel. On mobile, the controller scales to nearly full width, with key actions (Previous/Next) more prominent and centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal block element at the bottom of the listing; it scrolls into view after the user reaches the end of the current page of results. It does not stick or float, ensuring that the main visual emphasis stays on the hero, comparison summary, and package cards.

name: Package Comparison
title: Package Comparison
description: Side-by-side comparison of the user’s selected moving packages with options to remove packages and start a booking for any selected package.

core_features: Load the current PackageComparisonList and corresponding MovePackage records using getPackageComparisonDetails on page load.

Display all packages in the current comparison list in a side-by-side comparison layout, showing key MovePackage attributes such as name, service_type, move_scope, crew_size, includes_truck, base_hourly_rate, min_hours, estimated_total_4_hours (when provided), rating, review_count, tags, and included_add_on_ids.
Highlight differences between compared packages by dynamically detecting which MovePackage fields differ across the current set and visually marking those fields.
Allow users to remove individual packages from the current comparison list by calling removePackageFromComparisonList and then refreshing the comparison data.
Provide a clear per-package action to start a booking using startMoveBookingWithPackage, then navigate to booking.html with the appropriate URL parameters.
Handle and display an empty-state experience when the comparison list contains no packages, with navigation back to pricing.html so users can add packages to compare.
Optionally use getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary to update a compact summary (e.g., number of packages in comparison) without reloading full comparison details.
user_workflows:
1) View and compare packages
   1. User navigates to compare.html, optionally with a view URL parameter (e.g., compare.html?view=current).
   2. The page-controller component reads the view parameter and initializes view mode (e.g., whether to auto-enable difference highlighting).
   3. The page invokes getPackageComparisonDetails.
   4. On success, the returned comparison_list and packages are stored in page state.
   5. The comparison-table component renders one column per MovePackage, displaying all relevant fields side-by-side.
   6. The difference-highlighting logic compares field values across packages and marks any differing fields when the user enables this mode (or by default if dictated by the view parameter).
   7. The comparison-summary-bar shows a summary (e.g., count of compared packages) using the comparison_list data.

2) Remove a package from comparison
   1. Within the comparison-table, the user clicks a “Remove” action associated with a specific package.
   2. The table component triggers onRemovePackageClick with the selected package_id.
   3. The booking/removal handler component calls removePackageFromComparisonList(package_id).
   4. On success, the component updates local state with the returned comparison_list and re-invokes getPackageComparisonDetails to refresh the packages list.
   5. The UI re-renders with the updated set of packages.
   6. If no packages remain, the empty-comparison-state component replaces the comparison table and offers a navigation link back to pricing.html.

3) Start booking from a selected package
   1. In the comparison-table, the user clicks the “Book this package” action for a specific MovePackage.
   2. The table component triggers onBookPackageClick with the relevant package_id.
   3. The booking-start-handler component calls startMoveBookingWithPackage(package_id).
   4. If the response indicates success, the component uses the returned booking object as needed for client-side context and then navigates to booking.html with URL parameters source=pricing and package_id set to the selected package’s id.
   5. If the response indicates failure, the booking-start-handler exposes an error state, which the comparison-table displays near the relevant package or as a global error message.

4) Handle empty comparison state / return from other pages
   1. User navigates to compare.html when there are no packages currently stored in the PackageComparisonList for this session/context.
   2. getPackageComparisonDetails returns a comparison_list with an empty package_ids array and an empty packages array.
   3. The page-controller detects this state and renders the empty-comparison-state component instead of the comparison-table.
   4. The empty-comparison-state component provides a navigation action (e.g., a button) to go back to pricing.html so the user can select packages for comparison.
   5. When the user later adds packages from pricing.html and returns (e.g., via a “View comparison” control), the workflow follows the standard load-and-render steps.

5) Lightweight summary refresh (optional)
   1. While the user is on compare.html, the comparison-summary-bar or another small summary component may need to refresh its data without reloading all details.
   2. The summary component triggers onSummaryRefresh.
   3. The component calls getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary.
   4. On success, it updates the summary display (e.g., count of items in comparison_list.package_ids) without altering or re-fetching the current detailed packages list.
interactions: Click on “Remove from comparison” for a package: invokes onRemovePackageClick(package_id) in the comparison-table, which calls removePackageFromComparisonList and then triggers a refresh via getPackageComparisonDetails.

Click on “Book this package” for a package: invokes onBookPackageClick(package_id) in the comparison-table, which calls startMoveBookingWithPackage and, on success, navigates to booking.html?source=pricing&package_id=<selectedPackage.id>.

Toggle “Highlight differences only” (e.g., a switch or checkbox): invokes onToggleHighlightDifferences in the comparison-table, which updates internal view state to either show all attributes or visually emphasize/hide attributes that do not differ between packages (no API calls).
Change sorting (e.g., sort by hourly rate, rating): invokes onSortChange in the comparison-table, which reorders the in-memory packages array and re-renders the table without additional API calls.
Page load / URL change: invokes onPageInit or onViewParamChange in the page-controller, which reads the view parameter from the URL and calls getPackageComparisonDetails accordingly.

Click on a link to return to Pricing & Packages: invokes onNavigateToPricing in the empty-comparison-state (or summary) component, which navigates the browser to pricing.html, optionally preserving or setting URL parameters as required by that page.
Manual refresh of comparison summary (if implemented): invokes onSummaryRefresh in the comparison-summary-bar, which calls getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary and updates the summary view.
page_functionality.state_logic:
On initial load, the page-controller reads the view URL parameter from compare.html (e.g., compare.html?view=current). This parameter is used purely to adjust front-end behavior such as whether to enable difference highlighting by default or select a particular default sort mode; it does not change which packages are loaded. The actual set of compared packages is determined solely by the server-side PackageComparisonList returned by getPackageComparisonDetails.

The primary server-backed state on this page is the current PackageComparisonList and its associated MovePackage records. Page state includes:
- comparison_list: the PackageComparisonList object returned from getPackageComparisonDetails (or getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary in lightweight scenarios).
- packages: the array of MovePackage objects returned from getPackageComparisonDetails.
- viewMode: derived from the view URL parameter (e.g., 'current' or other values) and used to control client-side modes such as difference highlighting.
- uiState flags (e.g., highlightDifferencesEnabled, currentSortField, currentSortDirection) managed fully on the client without URL parameters or persistent storage.

Whenever removePackageFromComparisonList is called, the returned comparison_list replaces the existing comparison_list in state, and the page immediately calls getPackageComparisonDetails again to fetch the updated packages array. If the updated comparison_list has no package_ids, the page switches to rendering the empty-comparison-state component.

When startMoveBookingWithPackage succeeds, the booking object returned is used to confirm that a booking has been initiated. Navigation then occurs to booking.html with URL parameters source=pricing and package_id set to the selected package's id, matching the outgoing navigation specification. No additional persistent state is stored via localStorage; all persistent data relies on the backend through the assigned interfaces and URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, reads URL parameters, calls comparison-related interfaces, and manages high-level page state such as the current PackageComparisonList, MovePackage array, and view mode.
event_handlers: onPageInit: Triggered on initial load; reads the view URL parameter and calls getPackageComparisonDetails. On success, stores comparison_list and packages in page state and determines initial viewMode and UI flags based on the view parameter.

onViewParamChange: Triggered if the URL changes while on the page (e.g., via internal navigation that modifies view); re-reads the view parameter and updates viewMode and dependent UI flags without necessarily re-fetching data.
onComparisonDataRefresh: Triggered after any change to the comparison list (e.g., after removePackageFromComparisonList); calls getPackageComparisonDetails again and updates bound components.
onEmptyStateDetected: Triggered when comparison_list.package_ids is empty; instructs the view to hide the comparison-table and show the empty-comparison-state component.

components.functionality: Displays a compact summary of the current comparison list, such as the number of packages being compared, and optionally exposes a manual refresh control.
event_handlers: onSummaryDataBind: Triggered when comparison_list is updated by the page-controller; updates the summary display based on comparison_list.package_ids.
onSummaryRefresh: Optional; triggered when the user manually refreshes the summary. Calls getCurrentPackageComparisonSummary and updates the bound comparison_list (and any derived counts) on success.
onNavigateToPricing: Optional; when invoked (e.g., via a link), changes location to pricing.html, possibly including a view parameter understood by the pricing page.

components.functionality: Renders all MovePackage entries from the current comparison list in a side-by-side tabular or column layout, showing comparable attributes and providing per-package actions (remove and book). Also supports client-side sorting and difference highlighting.
event_handlers: onTableDataBind: Triggered when the page-controller updates the packages array or comparison_list; renders or re-renders the comparison columns.

onToggleHighlightDifferences: Triggered when the user toggles a control to highlight or hide non-differing attributes. Updates internal state to compute which MovePackage fields differ across all packages and re-renders accordingly (no API calls).

onSortChange: Triggered when the user selects a different sort option. Reorders the locally stored packages array based on selected sort criteria (e.g., base_hourly_rate, rating) and re-renders the table.

onRemovePackageClick: Triggered when the user clicks the remove action for a package. Calls removePackageFromComparisonList with the given package_id and, on success, notifies the comparison-page-controller via onComparisonDataRefresh.
onBookPackageClick: Triggered when the user clicks the book action for a package. Delegates to the booking-start-handler component (or calls its exposed function) with the selected package_id.

components.functionality: Handles the process of starting a booking for a selected package by calling startMoveBookingWithPackage, processing the response, and navigating to booking.html with the required URL parameters.
event_handlers: onStartBooking: Invoked (typically from comparison-table.onBookPackageClick) with a package_id. Calls startMoveBookingWithPackage(package_id).

onBookingSuccess: Triggered when startMoveBookingWithPackage returns success=true. Receives the booking object, optionally updates any transient UI state (e.g., disabling the clicked button), and then navigates to booking.html?source=pricing&package_id=<selectedPackage.id>.

onBookingError: Triggered when startMoveBookingWithPackage returns success=false or encounters an error. Stores an error message from the response (if provided) and exposes it back to the comparison-table or a global error display region.

components.functionality: Displays when there are no packages in the current PackageComparisonList. Explains that there are no items to compare and offers navigation back to Pricing & Packages.
event_handlers: onEmptyStateCheck: Triggered when comparison_list is updated. If comparison_list.package_ids is empty or packages length is zero, this component is shown instead of the comparison-table.
onNavigateToPricing: Triggered when the user clicks a call-to-action to choose packages. Navigates to pricing.html, optionally including context parameters expected by pricing.html.

components.functionality: Provides pure logic to compute which fields differ between MovePackage records, enabling the comparison-table to highlight differences. Operates purely on in-memory MovePackage data without calling any interfaces.
event_handlers: onComputeDifferences: Triggered whenever the packages array changes or when highlightDifferencesEnabled is toggled. Iterates through all MovePackage objects to determine which fields have varying values across the comparison set and returns a map of field names to boolean difference flags to the comparison-table.
onClearDifferences: Triggered when difference highlighting is disabled; resets any difference markers used by the comparison-table.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (likely around 1140–1200px) and large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the top-to-bottom, centered flow from the mockup: a dark, full-width hero band at the top with centered heading and supporting text for 'Package Comparison', followed by a white background content section. Inside that white section, content is stacked vertically: first a floating, pill-like comparison summary bar near the top center, then a prominent, wide rounded card that contains the comparison table and booking actions. All content sits within a centered max-width container (about 1140–1200px) aligned to an implicit 12-column grid, with generous side gutters.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the top-to-bottom, centered flow from the mockup: a dark, full-width hero band at the top with centered heading and supporting text for 'Package Comparison', followed by a white background content section. Inside that white section, content is stacked vertically: first a floating, pill-like comparison summary bar near the top center, then a prominent, wide rounded card that contains the comparison table and booking actions. All content sits within a centered max-width container (about 1140–1200px) aligned to an implicit 12-column grid, with generous side gutters.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section occupies roughly the top 45–55% of the viewport height with the photographic background. Below that, the comparison section uses a white background spanning the full width, but the main interactive area (summary bar + comparison card) is visually constrained to about 70–80% of the viewport width. Vertical spacing between hero, summary bar, and comparison card is generous (around 64–96px between major blocks, 32–48px inside them), matching the low-density marketing feel. The comparison card itself dominates the lower half of the initial viewport height, inviting users to scroll for additional rows as needed.
layout_strategies.content_density: Elements are arranged with low-to-medium density: short, scannable text in the hero; a clean, single-line summary bar; and a comparison table that uses ample row height, clear grouping, and comfortable padding (around 16–24px inside cells). No area feels cramped: there’s noticeable breathing room above and below headings, between sections, and around CTAs. The table avoids tight, data-heavy styling and instead feels like a guided marketing comparison with concise labels and clear differentiation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow echoes the mockup’s Z-pattern and central emphasis: the eye starts at the logo in the global nav (top-left), moves across to any existing nav CTAs (top-right), then down into the hero heading ('Compare Packages') in bright brand color at the center. From there, attention drops to the comparison summary bar, which stretches horizontally and visually anchors the start of the comparison experience. The eye then follows the vertical flow into the large orange comparison card, scanning left-to-right across package columns and then down the rows. CTAs for starting a booking sit at the bottom area of each package column, providing clear endpoints for each visual column scan while the summary bar and any 'Show differences only' controls help users refine focus.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The page opens with a full-width hero band that mirrors the original contact page: a darkened, desaturated photograph of moving activity fills the background, overlaid by centered, bold hero text in orange and white that introduces the package comparison. Below this, the background shifts to a clean white band. Slightly overlapping the visual transition between hero and white section, a wide, pill-shaped summary bar appears as a floating card, centered and spanning most of the content width, with subtle drop shadow and rounded corners. Beneath it, with a generous gap, a large, wide orange rounded rectangle card dominates the page center, echoing the mockup’s form card. Inside this card lives the package comparison grid, laid out on a 12-column system so that the feature list sits on the left and each selected package occupies one or more grid columns to the right. Booking actions are anchored at the bottom of each package column, using strong orange or green buttons. When there are no packages selected, the large card area instead shows a centered empty state module with a friendly illustration and a clear call-to-action to browse or add packages. Overall, the layout feels clean, modern, and slightly friendly: strong brand color blocks, rounded containers, and generous whitespace guide users smoothly from understanding that they’re comparing packages to actually starting a booking for the option that fits them best.

layout.overall_description: As users land on the Package Comparison page, they first see a dark, full-width hero with a desaturated moving-related photo. Centered over this is a bold orange headline like 'Compare Moving Packages' with a concise white subheading explaining that they can review details side-by-side and start a booking. As they scroll slightly, the background transitions into a crisp white section. Floating at the top of this white area, aligned to the center and spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width, is a pill-shaped summary bar card with a white background and soft shadow, summarizing how many packages are currently being compared and offering quick actions like 'Clear all' or 'Show differences only'. Below this, with around 64px of breathing space, sits a large orange rounded rectangle card that fills most of the main content width and extends down the page, visually anchoring the main interaction. Inside this card, the comparison grid is laid out: a leftmost column lists features and criteria, while each selected package occupies its own column to the right.
