On smaller screens, this component reflows beneath the search filter as a single-row, horizontally scrollable strip of pills, centered or left-aligned within the content column, so users can swipe through categories before scrolling down to the questions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the navigation column uses position: sticky within the white FAQ section, remaining pinned near the top of the viewport (just below the global nav and hero boundary) as the user scrolls through the right-hand question list. On smaller screens, it behaves as a standard scrolling horizontal pill list beneath the filter, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ contact CTA appears at the bottom of the FAQ content section, serving as the final, prominent action once users have browsed through the questions. After the last accordion item, there is a generous vertical gap—about 48–64px—to visually separate the FAQ list from the CTA. The CTA itself is centered within the main content container and takes the form of a bold, rounded navigation link styled like a button. It is sized to auto-fit its text (e.g., 'Still have questions? Contact us'), with 14–16px vertical and 32–40px horizontal padding, a large pill-shaped border-radius (24–999px), and a solid orange background using the brand’s primary accent color. The text is white and set in a medium-to-large, easy-to-tap size, creating a strong contrast against the white page background. Surrounding the button is ample white space on all sides, especially below (another 64–96px) before the page footer, making it a clear focal point at the end of the scroll. On all screen sizes, this CTA remains centered within the content column, aligning with the hero’s central emphasis and reinforcing a clear, singular next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the FAQ section, part of the normal scroll flow, always centered within the content container with no fixed or sticky behavior.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for viewing contact details and submitting general or topic-specific inquiries to the moving company, with guidance to quote or booking flows when appropriate.

core_features: Load and display dynamic contact information (phone numbers, email addresses, business hours, physical address) from getContactPageInfo.
Provide a contact inquiry form that supports general and topic-specific inquiries and submits via submitContactInquiry.
Preselect the inquiry topic based on the optional URL parameter `topic` when the page is accessed from other pages (About, Areas, FAQ, Home).
Allow users to navigate to the quote request page if they actually need a price estimate rather than a general contact.
Allow users who are ready to schedule a move to go directly to the booking page with the appropriate source parameter.
user_workflows: View contact details workflow: 1) User navigates to contact.html (optionally with ?topic=...). 2) On page load, the page-init component calls getContactPageInfo. 3) When getContactPageInfo resolves, the contact-info component displays phone numbers, email addresses, business hours, and the physical address. 4) User can click a phone number (to initiate a call in capable devices) or click an email address (to open their mail client) using the values returned by the API.

General contact inquiry workflow: 1) User opens contact.html without specifying a topic in the URL. 2) Page-init sets the default topic to a generic value (for example 'general') in internal state. 3) User enters their name, email, phone, and a message in the contact form. 4) User optionally changes the topic via the topic dropdown. 5) On form submit, the form component validates required fields. 6) If validation passes, the form component calls submitContactInquiry with name, email, phone, selected topic, and message. 7) On success, the status component displays a confirmation message and shows the returned ticket_id. 8) On failure, the status component shows an error state and the user can correct inputs and resubmit.

Topic-specific inquiry from another page workflow: 1) User is on about.html, areas.html, faq.html, or index.html and clicks a context-specific contact link (for example, about billing or service area). 2) That link navigates to contact.html with a URL parameter, e.g., contact.html?topic=billing or contact.html?topic=service_area. 3) On load, the page-init component reads the `topic` parameter and sets the initial topic state to that value if it is recognized. 4) The contact form topic dropdown is preselected accordingly and may show a contextual label or hint that this inquiry will be routed with that topic. 5) User fills in name, email, phone, message and submits; submitContactInquiry is invoked with the preselected topic. 6) The status component shows success or error as in the general workflow.

Redirect to quote request workflow: 1) A user reaches contact.html intending to ask about prices. 2) Near the contact form, the user sees a functional link or button labeled to indicate that for price estimates they should use the quote request flow. 3) User clicks this link. 4) The click triggers navigation to quote.html without additional parameters (or with only those that might be needed by quote.html, not controlled here). 5) No calls to submitContactInquiry are made in this path unless the user already submitted the contact form before navigating away.

Redirect to booking workflow: 1) A user on contact.html is ready to schedule a move rather than ask a question. 2) The user clicks a link or button that indicates they can book a move now. 3) The click triggers navigation to booking.html with the URL parameter source=direct (e.g., booking.html?source=direct). 4) The booking page can then interpret source=direct as a booking initiated without a prior quote or estimator step. 5) No booking or quote-related data is created on the Contact page; only navigation occurs.
interactions: On page load, the page-init logic parses the URL to read the `topic` query parameter, normalizes it, and stores it in page state as the initial selected topic (if valid).
On page load, after topic initialization, the page invokes getContactPageInfo; while data is loading, dependent components can show a loading state or remain empty.
Clicking a phone number in the contact info section triggers a standard tel: navigation using the dynamically loaded number, allowing devices that support calling to initiate a call.
Clicking an email address in the contact info section triggers a standard mailto: navigation using the dynamically loaded email, opening the user’s email client.
Changing the topic selection in the topic dropdown updates the current topic in the form’s local state, overriding any previously set value from the URL parameter.
Typing into the name, email, phone, and message fields updates the form’s local state; basic inline validation can run on blur or on submit (e.g., required fields, basic email format).
Submitting the contact form triggers client-side validation; if validation fails, the form prevents the submitContactInquiry call and surfaces field-level errors in state.

If validation passes, submitting the form calls submitContactInquiry with the current field values; the form switches to a submitting state (e.g., disabling the submit button) until the API response is received.

On successful submitContactInquiry response (success=true), the form and status components store the returned ticket_id and API message in state and display a confirmation area; optionally, the form fields may be reset.
On failed submitContactInquiry response (success=false) or network error, the status component displays an error message and the form exits the submitting state so the user can retry.

Clicking the “Request a quote instead” navigation element near the form navigates to quote.html using standard link navigation without modifying any local state beyond what the browser naturally discards on navigation.

Clicking the “Book a move now” navigation element navigates to booking.html with the query parameter source=direct; this is generated dynamically in the link’s href and does not depend on additional state.

Reloading the page (browser refresh) re-runs the URL parameter parsing and getContactPageInfo; any unsaved form inputs from the previous load are lost since no state is persisted outside the current page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page state is driven by URL parameters and in-memory component state. On initial load, the page reads the `topic` query parameter from the URL (e.g., contact.html?topic=billing). If the parameter value matches one of the supported topics (such as 'general', 'service_area', 'billing', 'other' as defined by the application), it is used as the initial selected topic in the page-level state. If the parameter is missing or invalid, a default topic value is used instead. This topic state is then passed down to the topic selector control and to the contact form component as its initial topic. If the user changes the topic via the UI, that change is stored only in in-memory state and does not automatically update the URL; a page refresh will therefore revert the topic back to whatever is currently in the URL (or to the default if none is specified). Separately, after topic initialization, getContactPageInfo is called once and its response is stored in in-memory state; components that display phone numbers, email addresses, business hours, and physical address bind to this state. No assumptions are made about the number or content of items; all are rendered based on the API response. When the contact form is submitted, the form constructs the payload for submitContactInquiry from its current local field state (name, email, phone, topic, message). The response from submitContactInquiry (success flag, ticket_id, and message) is stored in in-memory state for display by the status component. The URL is not modified as a result of submission; reloading the page will not preserve the submitted state or ticket reference unless the surrounding application chooses to pass those via URL or another mechanism (not defined here). All persistent navigation to other flows (quote, booking) is handled via standard URL navigation, with booking links explicitly adding the parameter source=direct as required.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact page by reading the URL parameters, deriving the initial inquiry topic, and triggering the load of contact information via getContactPageInfo.

Displays the list of phone numbers, email addresses, business hours, and the physical address returned from getContactPageInfo, enabling click-to-call and click-to-email interactions based on the dynamic values.
Manages the currently selected inquiry topic. Initializes its value from the URL-derived topic state set by contact-page-init and allows the user to change the topic before submitting the form.

Collects user inputs for name, email, phone, topic, and message, validates them, and submits them to the backend via submitContactInquiry. Handles loading/submitting state and passes the resulting status to the status component.
Displays the outcome of a contact inquiry submission, including success or error state, the API-provided message, and the ticket_id when available.

Provides alternative navigation options near the contact form: one link to the quote request page for users who need a price estimate, and another to the booking page for users ready to schedule a move, including the required source=direct parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (likely around 1140–1200px) and large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a strong center alignment. At the very top, the contact page initializes into a full-width hero with a darkened photographic background and a floating, centered navigation bar card. Hero text (page title and short explanation) is centrally aligned both horizontally and roughly vertically within the upper half of the viewport. Below the hero, the background switches to solid white. In this white section, the main section heading and contact info are positioned toward the left of the central content column, followed by a large, wide orange rounded card centered beneath them that contains the inquiry form, topic selector, and status banner. Below the form card, a compact, centered alternate navigation link group offers other flows (quote, booking, etc.). All components sit inside a max-width content container (around 1140–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a strong center alignment. At the very top, the contact page initializes into a full-width hero with a darkened photographic background and a floating, centered navigation bar card. Hero text (page title and short explanation) is centrally aligned both horizontally and roughly vertically within the upper half of the viewport. Below the hero, the background switches to solid white. In this white section, the main section heading and contact info are positioned toward the left of the central content column, followed by a large, wide orange rounded card centered beneath them that contains the inquiry form, topic selector, and status banner. Below the form card, a compact, centered alternate navigation link group offers other flows (quote, booking, etc.). All components sit inside a max-width content container (around 1140–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies approximately 45–55% of the initial viewport height, dominated by the background image and floating nav. The floating nav card spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width with large side margins. Below the hero, the white content section has generous top padding (about 80–96px) before the 'Reach Out To Us' heading and contact info. The large orange form card beneath takes up about 70–80% of the content width (8–10 of 12 columns), visually dominating the lower half of the above-the-fold area on typical laptop screens. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → white section → form → alt navigation) sits in the 64–96px range, with 32–48px padding inside the card and 16–24px between internal elements (labels, fields, status banner).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low-to-medium. The hero section uses minimal text with ample breathing room between the nav, the main heading, and the subheading. The contact info area uses short, scannable blocks (phone, email, hours) with generous line spacing. The form card uses a vertical layout with clear grouping and 16–24px spacing between form controls, ensuring elements don’t feel cramped. The status banner and topic selector sit cleanly within the form without overcrowding, and the alternate navigation links at the bottom are concise and spaced apart to maintain the airy feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the floating nav in the hero: logo on the left, navigation links across the middle, and a bright orange phone/CTA button on the right, creating a Z-pattern across the header. The eye then drops to the central hero heading 'Contact Us' in strong orange, followed by the smaller white supporting text. Scrolling down, attention moves to the left-aligned 'Reach Out To Us' heading and the compact contact info beneath it. From there, the eye is pulled to the large, high-contrast orange rounded form card centered on the page. Within that card, the flow is top-down: optional status banner, then topic selector dropdown, then the main inquiry form fields, and finally the primary submit action. Below that, visual focus softens to the alternate navigation link group, centered and slightly smaller, guiding users to quote/booking paths without competing with the main form.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page opens on a full-width hero with a dark, desaturated photo background that fills roughly half the viewport. Floating over the upper portion of this image is a wide, pill-shaped white navigation card, centered horizontally with generous side margins and a subtle shadow, containing the logo on the left, primary nav links across the middle, and a prominent orange phone CTA on the right. Below this, in the center of the hero, a bold orange 'Contact Us' heading and a short white supporting line are stacked and centrally aligned, standing out clearly against the darkened image. As you scroll past the hero, the background transitions sharply to pure white and a centered content column appears. At the top-left of this column is the section heading 'Reach Out To Us', followed by succinct contact details aligned to the left, occupying the left side of the column. Beneath this, a large orange rounded rectangle card is centered within the content width, spanning most of the column and slightly elevated with soft corners and shadow—this card houses the core enquiry experience: a title, an optional status banner, the topic selector dropdown, and the main inquiry form fields stacked in a clean vertical flow. Below the card, after a generous vertical gap, a compact row of alternate navigation links is centered on the page, offering access to quote and booking flows in a light, unobtrusive way. Overall, the layout feels clean, modern, and approachable, with clear visual hierarchy and a strong funnel toward completing the contact form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component defines the entire hero section, starting at the very top of the page. It spans the full browser width and uses a dark, desaturated photographic background that extends to roughly 45–55% of the viewport height on initial load. Centered within this hero is a max-width content container (about 1140–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid. Within the top part of this container, a floating navigation bar appears as a large, pill-shaped white card. This nav card is horizontally centered, leaving generous left and right margins (it spans about 70–80% of the viewport width), and sits roughly 24–32px from the top edge of the viewport. The card has a height of about 72–88px, with 16–24px internal vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Inside it, the logo is anchored to the far left, navigation links are arranged horizontally in the center, and a bright orange CTA button (e.g., phone or 'Call Now') is right-aligned, creating a clear top-level Z-pattern. Below the nav card, the hero’s main text block is vertically centered between the nav bar and the lower edge of the hero. This text block is center-aligned and limited to roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (around 60–70% of the container width). The primary heading 'Contact Us' is displayed in a large, bold orange type, centered on the page, with about 16–24px of space below it before a smaller, white subheading line that explains what users can do on this page. There is ample negative space above and below the text block. The bottom of the hero section transitions to the white content area with about 48–64px of vertical breathing room, ensuring the floating nav and hero text feel distinct from the section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The hero background is fixed to the page scroll in a standard manner (non-sticky), while the navigation card visually floats over it but scrolls away with the rest of the content. The entire component participates in the normal document flow as the top-most section, occupying full width and approximately half the initial viewport height without any sticky or fixed behavior for its internal elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-section sits immediately below the hero, in the first white background section of the page. It is contained within the same centered max-width content area (around 1140–1200px) and aligned to the underlying 12-column grid. There is a substantial vertical gap (about 80–96px) between the bottom of the hero and the top of this section’s content, reinforcing the spacious feel. At the top of this section, the heading—such as 'Reach Out To Us'—is placed toward the left side of the main content column. It starts roughly at column 2 and extends to about column 7 (roughly 40–60% of the container width), left-aligned, with 8–16px padding above and below in line with the typographic scale. Directly beneath the heading, the contact info details are presented in a compact, left-aligned block that occupies about 4–5 of the 12 columns (around 35–45% of the container width). This block includes key details like phone number, email, office address, and operating hours, each on its own line or grouped in small clusters with 8–16px spacing between items. The right side of the content column remains mostly open, preserving generous white space and visually setting up the area where the large orange form card will appear below. The vertical space between this contact info block and the top edge of the upcoming orange card is about 32–48px, allowing the contact info to feel related to, but not merged with, the form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component flows normally within the document, with no fixed or sticky behavior. On wider screens it uses a left column layout within the central container, while on smaller screens (tablet and mobile) it scales down to full width, stacking the heading and contact details vertically and maintaining generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-topic-selector is positioned inside the main orange form card, near the top of the form content area. Within the form card’s internal grid, it sits as a full-width row in the central column stack, aligned with other form fields. Vertically, the topic selector appears below any optional form title and the status banner (if present), but above the primary text input fields (such as name, email, message). There is a consistent vertical spacing of about 16–24px above and below the dropdown to match other form field spacing. The control itself spans roughly 100% of the available form width on mobile and about 8–10 of 12 internal columns (equivalent to 70–80% of the card width) on desktop, centered or left-aligned depending on the form’s alignment choice. A label such as 'What do you need help with?' is left-aligned directly above the dropdown, with 4–8px of space between label and field. The control is visually substantial enough to be quickly noticed, but still reads as one of the form fields rather than a separate section. Its placement at the top of the field stack encourages users to classify their inquiry before entering detailed information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The topic selector behaves as a standard inline form field inside the card, participating in the normal vertical flow of inputs. It does not fix or float; instead, it expands or contracts horizontally with the form card across breakpoints, maintaining full-width on small screens and a comfortable column width on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-inquiry-form is expressed as the large, visually dominant orange rounded rectangle card in the white section. It is positioned directly below the contact-info-section, centered within the same max-width container. The top edge of the card sits approximately 32–48px beneath the contact info, creating a clear yet connected relationship. On desktop, the card spans about 8–10 of the 12 grid columns (roughly 70–80% of the content width) and is centered horizontally, leaving generous white space on both sides. The card has large corner radii (around 24–32px) and a soft drop shadow that lifts it slightly from the white background, reinforcing its importance. Internally, the card has 32–40px padding on all sides (24px on smaller screens). The internal layout is a vertical stack of elements: an optional small header or title (e.g., 'Send Us a Message') aligned to the top-left inside the card, followed by the status banner (when visible), then the topic selector, followed by the main set of form fields (name, contact details, message, etc.), and finally a prominent submit button area at the bottom. On wider screens, some fields may align in two-column rows (e.g., name and phone side-by-side), but the overall layout maintains vertical clarity and 16–24px gaps between rows. The primary submit action is either right-aligned at the bottom of the card or centered, taking up roughly 25–35% of the card width on desktop and full width on mobile. The internal field labels and texts are white or lightly tinted for high contrast against the orange background while maintaining the modern, friendly corporate style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form card is a standard block-level element within the white section, visually floating via shadow but not physically fixed or sticky. It adapts responsively: on large screens it remains centered and wide; on smaller screens it expands to nearly the full width of the viewport with shorter horizontal margins, while preserving its padding, corner radius, and vertical hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-inquiry-status component appears inside the orange form card, immediately below any optional form title and above the contact-topic-selector. It occupies the full internal width of the card, stretching edge-to-edge inside the card’s padding. Visually, it reads as a horizontal banner strip or pill with a slightly different tone or overlay compared to the main orange background—e.g., a lighter or darker orange, or a green accent for success states. The banner has rounded corners that echo the card’s radius but scaled down (e.g., 8–12px), and 12–16px of internal padding. It contains a small status icon on the left plus a short status message text to its right, both aligned vertically in the middle. There is approximately 16–24px of space between the bottom of this status banner and the topic selector field below, so that when it is visible it clearly separates from the input controls without cluttering. When hidden (no status), this space collapses so the topic selector shifts up, maintaining an efficient but still spacious layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This banner is an inline, conditional element within the form card’s vertical stack. It appears and disappears based on form submission or validation state, pushing other content down or allowing it to pull up accordingly. It does not stick to the top of the viewport; it moves with the form as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-alt-navigation component is placed below the orange form card within the same white background section. After the bottom edge of the form card, there is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px, then this link group appears centered in the content container. It is constrained to roughly 6–8 of the 12 grid columns (about 50–65% of the container width) and is either center-aligned or slightly left-aligned but still centered as a block. At the top of this component, there may be a small heading or prompt such as 'Need something else?' or 'Other ways to get started', with 8–16px margin beneath it. Below that, the alternate paths (e.g., 'Get a Moving Quote', 'Book a Move', 'View FAQs') are presented as a horizontal row of 2–4 links or pill-shaped buttons, spaced 16–24px apart. On large screens, these links sit side-by-side in a single row; on smaller screens, they wrap into two rows while staying centered. The links visually read as secondary actions (e.g., outlined buttons, green text links, or underlined text) so they don’t compete with the main form CTA but remain easy to discover once the user has scanned or completed the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component is a standard block element at the end of the contact content, following the form in normal document flow. It is not fixed or sticky and simply appears as the user scrolls past the form. Its layout is responsive, adjusting the link arrangement from a single-row horizontal layout on desktop to a stacked or wrapped configuration on tablet and mobile while preserving central alignment.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Displays the legal terms governing use of the moving website and services, with section-based navigation, deep-linking via URL parameters, and a link to the Privacy Policy.

core_features: Load Terms & Conditions content dynamically via getTermsAndConditionsContent, including last updated date and an array of sections.
Provide a section-based table of contents that lets users jump to any legal section on the page.
Support deep-linking to specific sections using the URL parameter `section`, including when arriving from booking, promotions, or insurance pages.
Highlight and scroll to the active section based on the `section` URL parameter and user clicks.
Allow users to copy or share a URL that opens the page directly on a specific section by updating the `section` parameter when they navigate.
Handle in-text links to the Privacy Policy so that clicking “View our Privacy Policy” (or equivalent link) navigates to privacy.html.
Gracefully handle loading and error states when fetching Terms & Conditions content.
user_workflows:
Workflow: General visit from footer
1) User clicks the “Terms & Conditions” link in the footer (no URL params).
2) Page loads and calls getTermsAndConditionsContent.
3) Once data is loaded, the table of contents lists all sections and the full content is rendered.
4) The first section in the list becomes the active section by default.
5) User clicks different section titles in the table of contents to navigate; the `section` URL parameter updates accordingly.

Workflow: Deep-link from booking / promotions / insurance
1) User clicks a link such as “View cancellation terms” on booking.html, promotions.html, or insurance.html.
2) Browser opens terms.html with a `section` URL parameter matching the referenced legal section.
3) Page loads and calls getTermsAndConditionsContent.
4) After sections are available, the page looks for a section whose `id` matches the `section` URL parameter.
5) If a matching section is found, that section is marked active and scrolled into view; if not, the default behavior (e.g., first section) is used.
6) User can still switch sections via the table of contents; the URL `section` parameter is updated so that a refresh or share will keep the same section.

Workflow: User reads details and navigates to Privacy Policy
1) User scrolls and reads through Terms & Conditions sections.
2) Within the body of one section, the text includes a link labeled similarly to “View our Privacy Policy”.
3) User clicks this link inside the terms content area.
4) The click is intercepted by the page’s link handler, which detects that the link targets the privacy policy.
5) Browser navigates to privacy.html (keeping any required query parameters if defined by the link).

Workflow: User shares a link to a specific section
1) User navigates to a particular section using the table of contents.
2) When the section changes, the page updates the browser URL to include the appropriate `section` parameter via the History API.
3) User copies the URL from the browser’s address bar.
4) Another person opening that URL lands on terms.html with `section` set; after loading, the page automatically scrolls to and highlights that same section.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data load
- Trigger: Page load event.
- Behavior: The data-loader component calls getTermsAndConditionsContent with no parameters.
- Result: On success, the page state stores `last_updated` and `sections` and notifies child components to render. On failure, an error message state is set and the main content area shows an error state.

Interaction: Table of contents click
- Trigger: User clicks a section entry in the table of contents.
- Behavior: The click handler reads the associated section `id`, updates the active section state, scrolls the corresponding section into view, and updates the `section` URL parameter using history.pushState/replaceState.
- Result: The selected section is visually marked as active and remains the active section on refresh or when the URL is shared.

Interaction: URL parameter change via browser navigation
- Trigger: User presses the browser Back or Forward button, changing the URL’s `section` parameter.
- Behavior: A popstate listener reads the new `section` value, updates the active section state, and scrolls to the matching section if it exists.
- Result: The visible active section in the UI matches the `section` parameter reflected in the browser URL history.

Interaction: Auto-activation of section on first load
- Trigger: Data from getTermsAndConditionsContent is received.
- Behavior: The state manager checks the current URL for a `section` parameter. If present and matching a section `id`, that section is activated and scrolled into view. If not present or not matching, the first section in the loaded list is set as active (without altering any application-wide static content).
- Result: Users arriving from deep links see the relevant section first; other users start from a predictable default section.

Interaction: In-text link click within terms content (including Privacy Policy link)
- Trigger: User clicks an anchor element within the rendered `body_html` of any section.
- Behavior: A delegated click handler inspects the clicked link. If the href targets privacy.html (or an equivalent designated privacy URL), the handler allows or initiates navigation to privacy.html. For other links, the handler can either allow normal navigation or, if the link is to another section within the same page (e.g., via a section id), prevent default navigation and instead update the active section and `section` URL parameter.
- Result: Privacy Policy links navigate to privacy.html; intra-terms links update which section is active without full page reload.

Interaction: Error/empty-state handling
- Trigger: getTermsAndConditionsContent returns an error or a payload without usable sections.
- Behavior: The data-loader sets an error or empty state. The content area displays a functional message derived from that state (no static legal text) and hides or disables the table of contents and section viewer.
- Result: Users receive feedback that terms could not be loaded and are prevented from interacting with missing content.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary piece of state is `activeSectionId`, which is synchronized with the URL parameter `section`. On initial load, the URL is parsed: if `section` exists, its value is stored as the desired active section; otherwise, no specific section is requested. The page then calls getTermsAndConditionsContent. When the sections are loaded, the state manager checks whether any section’s `id` matches the `section` parameter value. If a match is found, `activeSectionId` is set to that value and the content scrolls to that section; if not, `activeSectionId` is set to the first available section’s `id` without adding assumptions about its meaning, while leaving the URL unchanged or optionally updating it to reflect this default. Whenever the user selects a different section (via table of contents or an in-text link), `activeSectionId` is updated and the browser URL is updated with `?section=<newId>` using history.pushState or history.replaceState; no localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used. A popstate listener keeps the UI in sync with history navigation: when the user uses Back/Forward and the `section` parameter changes, the listener updates `activeSectionId` and scrolls accordingly. No static term names, IDs, or legal content are hardcoded in the page; all section identifiers, titles, and bodies come from getTermsAndConditionsContent.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches Terms & Conditions data using getTermsAndConditionsContent, manages loading and error states, and exposes `last_updated` and `sections` to child components.

Coordinates the `activeSectionId` state based on URL parameter `section`, loaded sections, and user navigation actions (clicks and browser history events). Ensures that the correct section is highlighted and scrolled into view.

Renders a navigable list of all sections returned by getTermsAndConditionsContent (using each section’s `title` and `id`). Highlights the currently active section and allows users to click to change the active section.

Displays the full Terms & Conditions content by rendering each section’s `body_html` and `title`. Sets DOM anchors or identifiers based on section `id` for scrolling and deep-linking. Delegates click handling for in-text links, including anchors pointing to other sections or to the privacy policy.
Shows the Terms & Conditions last-updated information using the `last_updated` field from getTermsAndConditionsContent. Updates automatically when new data is loaded.

Parses the `section` URL parameter on load and on browser history events, and updates the `activeSectionId` in cooperation with the state manager. When `activeSectionId` changes due to user interactions, updates the browser URL’s `section` parameter via the History API, without reloading the page.

Listens for click events on anchor elements within the Terms & Conditions content area. When a clicked link targets the Privacy Policy (e.g., via an href pointing to privacy.html or a designated marker), it initiates navigation to privacy.html. For other in-text links, it may delegate to the section state manager if the link targets another terms section.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (likely around 1140–1200px) and large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom centered flow as the mockup. At the top sits the existing site nav and a hero band with a darkened photographic background, over which a centered page title ('Terms & Conditions') and a short supporting line are vertically and horizontally centered. Below the hero, the background switches to solid white and all legal content is contained within a max-width (≈1140–1200px) centered column. Inside this column, the legal heading row (page title + last updated indicator) appears first, and directly beneath it a two-column layout begins: a compact Terms Table of Contents on the left and the main Terms sections on the right. On smaller screens, these reorder into a single column with the TOC stacked above the content, preserving a linear reading order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom centered flow as the mockup. At the top sits the existing site nav and a hero band with a darkened photographic background, over which a centered page title ('Terms & Conditions') and a short supporting line are vertically and horizontally centered. Below the hero, the background switches to solid white and all legal content is contained within a max-width (≈1140–1200px) centered column. Inside this column, the legal heading row (page title + last updated indicator) appears first, and directly beneath it a two-column layout begins: a compact Terms Table of Contents on the left and the main Terms sections on the right. On smaller screens, these reorder into a single column with the TOC stacked above the content, preserving a linear reading order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 45–55% of the viewport height with generous breathing room around the title text. The legal content section below uses large vertical padding (≈64–96px top and bottom) and generous lateral gutters. Within the content area, the left TOC column uses about 25–30% of the central container width, while the right section list uses 70–75%. Individual section blocks have 32–48px vertical spacing between them, and inner padding of 24–32px inside the main content card, echoing the comfortable spacing from the orange card in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is low-to-medium. The TOC presents a concise list of section titles with ample line spacing, and the main terms content is broken into clearly separated sections with readable line lengths and 24–32px spacing between headings and paragraphs. No part of the layout feels cramped; whitespace around and between the TOC, the last-updated indicator, and the content card mirrors the airy marketing feel of the original mockup while accommodating more text-heavy legal content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the site logo in the nav, across any nav links to the right, then down into the hero center where the 'Terms & Conditions' title sits. As the user scrolls past the hero, attention drops to the left-aligned section heading and immediately to the right-side 'Last updated' indicator. From there, the eye is drawn into the left TOC card (which offers quick orientation), then across to the main content card on the right, where the first section heading and body text reside. As the user scrolls through the terms, the sticky TOC stays in the periphery, with active states and URL syncing gently guiding attention to the current section. Toward the end, the Privacy Policy link inside the content is highlighted in brand orange, pulling attention to the related document.

layout.overall_description: The Terms & Conditions page sits within the same visual system as the rest of the moving company site: a floating, rounded white nav bar at the very top, followed by a full-width hero featuring a dark, slightly desaturated photo of a moving scene. Centered in the hero, a bold orange page title ('Terms & Conditions') and a short white supporting line are vertically aligned in the upper half of the viewport, occupying about half the screen height before the user scrolls. As the hero gives way, the background switches to pure white, and a wide but constrained content container (roughly 1140–1200px) appears centered on the page, framed by generous side gutters. The first row in this content area holds the left-aligned page heading and introductory line, with a small, right-aligned 'Last updated' indicator sitting on the same horizontal line, giving a clear legal timestamp. Below, a soft-elevated, rounded content zone spans most of the width. Within this zone, the left third is a vertically oriented Table of Contents in a pill-edged white card, and the right two-thirds is a large, rounded white content card that houses all terms sections. The TOC remains visible and sticky as the user scrolls, while the main content flows downward in well-spaced sections. When the content first loads, a subtle overlay loader briefly appears centered over the terms card. Anchored deep links update the URL as each section comes into view, and an inline brand-colored link near the bottom leads to the Privacy Policy. Overall, the page feels calm, clear, and professional, with modern rounded shapes and brand accents, while keeping the legal content highly readable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader appears only over the main terms content area, not the entire viewport. It is positioned centered within the right-side content card that holds the section list. Visually, it is a semi-transparent white overlay that covers 100% of the content card’s width and height, with a subtle blur or dimming effect to indicate a loading state while still hinting at the card shape beneath. In the middle of this overlay, a small circular spinner or progress indicator sits exactly in the horizontal and vertical center of the card. The overlay respects the rounded corners of the underlying card, following its 16–24px radius. There is at least 32px of whitespace between the overlay’s edges and the card’s outer margin to keep the loader feeling contained and unobtrusive. On smaller screens, the overlay still covers only the content area, scaling to the full width of the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned within the bounds of the main content card, stretching to cover it edge-to-edge while visible. It scrolls with the page but is only rendered during loading states, then completely removed so it does not affect normal layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section state manager has no visual presence on the page; it operates behind the scenes in tandem with scroll and navigation interactions. Conceptually, it is anchored to the vertical flow of the content column, tracking which section in the right-hand content card is currently nearest the top of the viewport and signaling that state to the TOC on the left (e.g., to highlight the active section). Its influence is most apparent in how the left TOC link styling changes as the user scrolls through the stacked sections on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, logical layer tied to scroll position and section anchors; it does not occupy physical space and has no direct spatial behavior beyond reacting to viewport scroll and section positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Terms Table of Contents sits within the main white background section directly below the hero, aligned inside the centered max-width content container. On desktop, it occupies the left 25–30% of the content width, starting about 64px below the transition from the hero and roughly aligned vertically with the top of the main content card on the right. It appears as a tall, rounded-rectangle card (border-radius ≈16–20px) with a white background, subtle shadow, and 24px padding on all sides. The list of section titles is stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between items, and an active item is emphasized using the brand orange (e.g., left border or text color) while inactive items are in dark gray. The top of the TOC card aligns with the first line of the main terms content so they feel like a single, coordinated module. As the user scrolls down, the TOC becomes sticky: it remains fixed within the left column, maintaining a 96px offset from the top of the viewport, while the right column content scrolls. On tablets and smaller devices, the TOC stretches to full width above the content, becoming a horizontal pill-like band with scrollable chips or stacked links, and loses the sticky behavior in favor of a simple top-of-content placement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger viewports, it is a left-column element that becomes sticky after the user scrolls past its initial position, maintaining visibility while respecting a top offset to avoid overlapping the nav. On smaller viewports, it behaves like a standard block element stacked above the content, scrolling normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section list represents the main Terms & Conditions content and occupies the right side of the content area. On desktop, it spans about 70–75% of the central container’s width and begins on the same vertical line as the TOC’s top edge, with a consistent 24–32px horizontal gap between the two columns. Visually, it is contained within a large, rounded white card that echoes the form-like card from the mockup but uses neutral white for readability. The card’s border-radius is around 16–20px with a subtle shadow and 24–32px internal padding. Inside, each terms section is stacked vertically, with section headings in a strong, dark type and 16–24px spacing before the following paragraphs. There is 32–48px of vertical spacing between each major section to create clear separation. Section anchors (for URL deep-linking) are aligned at the top of each section heading, with a few pixels of offset accounted for to ensure headings are fully visible below the sticky nav and TOC when navigated via in-page links. The card extends downward as far as needed, creating a continuous scroll experience within the overall page scroll. On smaller screens, this card expands to full width below the TOC, preserving the same internal spacing but removing the two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard scrollable content block within the page. It is not fixed or sticky; instead, the card’s top edge scrolls off-screen while the TOC (on larger screens) remains visible. The internal anchor points enable smooth scrolling when TOC items or URL deep links are activated.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last updated indicator sits in the same horizontal band as the page heading at the top of the white content section, just below the hero. Within the central max-width container, the main text heading (e.g., 'Terms & Conditions') is left-aligned near the start of the content area, and the last updated indicator is aligned to the right edge of this same row, roughly 64px below the bottom of the hero. The spacing between the hero and this row follows the 64–96px section margin, creating a clear visual break. The indicator is a compact, single-line text element (for example, 'Last updated: Jan 12, 2026') in a muted gray with smaller font size than the heading, possibly accompanied by a small calendar icon. It does not sit inside the main content card but above it, in the open white space of the section header, with about 16px of vertical clearance before the start of the TOC and main content card below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow, moving upward as the user scrolls. It does not stick or float; it simply anchors the top of the legal content and provides a timestamp aligned with the page heading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL sync controller is non-visual and sits conceptually behind the two-column content layout. It associates each TOC entry on the left with a corresponding anchored section in the right-hand content card. When the user clicks a TOC item or scrolls to a new section, the browser’s URL updates to include the relevant section identifier without a full page reload. Its presence is perceived through how the URL and browser history respond to navigation and scrolling, not through any visible element on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that reacts to scroll position and click events; it does not occupy physical space or change layout, but it is logically bound to the top offsets of the section headings to ensure accurate deep-linking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy link handler manages interactions with the Privacy Policy link embedded within one of the lower terms sections inside the main content card. Visually, the link itself appears inline within a paragraph of the section list, typically toward the end of the document where related policies are referenced. The text is underlined or distinct and uses the brand orange color to stand out against the otherwise neutral body text. There is 4–8px of whitespace around the line height so the link is easy to tap on touch devices. The handler itself is invisible; it ensures that when the user activates this link, navigation behavior (e.g., opening in the same tab with a smooth scroll to a specific section, or tracking analytics) is consistent with the rest of the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavior bound to the inline link within the content; it does not alter layout or positioning, but it controls navigation behavior when the link is clicked or tapped.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that dynamically loads and displays the website’s Privacy Policy, organized into sections with deep-link navigation using the `section` URL parameter.

core_features: Load privacy policy content via `getPrivacyPolicyContent` on page initialization, including the last-updated timestamp and an ordered list of sections.
Render each policy section (from `getPrivacyPolicyContent.sections`) with its own in-page anchor keyed by the section’s `id`, allowing precise navigation and scrolling.
Generate a dynamic table of contents from `getPrivacyPolicyContent.sections` so users can click to jump to any section; clicks update the `section` URL parameter.
Support deep-linking from other pages using `privacy.html?section={sectionId}` by reading the `section` URL parameter on load and focusing the corresponding section.
Respond to changes in the `section` URL parameter (including browser back/forward) by updating which section is scrolled into view and visually emphasized.
Display the policy’s `last_updated` value from `getPrivacyPolicyContent` so users can see when the policy was last changed, without any hardcoded dates.
Provide per-section direct-link actions (e.g., a link or button) that update the URL to include `?section={id}` to make it easy for users to share or return to specific privacy topics.
user_workflows: Read the full Privacy Policy (no specific section requested): 1) User navigates to privacy.html from a footer or form link without a `section` parameter; 2) The page controller calls `getPrivacyPolicyContent`; 3) While loading, the page shows a generic loading state; 4) When data returns, the header displays the policy title and `last_updated` value, the table of contents is generated from the returned `sections`, and the full list of sections is rendered; 5) No specific section is focused because no `section` URL parameter is present.

Open the Privacy Policy focused on a specific topic from another page: 1) User clicks a link on another page (e.g., from a consent notice) that points to privacy.html with `?section={sectionId}`; 2) On load, the page controller reads the `section` URL parameter and calls `getPrivacyPolicyContent`; 3) When the sections are loaded, the focus manager looks for a section whose `id` matches the `section` parameter; 4) If found, the page automatically scrolls to that section and applies a visual focus state; 5) The table of contents reflects the same active section.

Navigate between different privacy topics using the table of contents: 1) User arrives on privacy.html and content is loaded via `getPrivacyPolicyContent`; 2) User clicks a section entry in the table of contents; 3) The click handler updates the browser URL to include `?section={clickedSectionId}` (using pushState or similar) and triggers a section-change event; 4) The focus manager scrolls the matching section into view and updates the active state in the table of contents; 5) The user can use browser back/forward to move between previously selected sections because each selection updated the URL.

Share or save a link to a specific Privacy Policy section: 1) After content is loaded via `getPrivacyPolicyContent`, each rendered section includes a direct-link control (e.g., a small link/button near the section title); 2) User clicks this control for a particular section; 3) The click handler updates the current page URL to ensure `?section={thisSectionId}` is present, and may optionally copy that full URL to the clipboard using browser APIs; 4) User can share that URL or bookmark it; 5) When someone later opens that URL, the page loads via `getPrivacyPolicyContent` and focuses the same section in accordance with the `section` parameter.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads the `section` URL parameter (if present) and calls `getPrivacyPolicyContent`; when the promise resolves, it populates the header, table of contents, and section list.

If `getPrivacyPolicyContent` fails (e.g., network or server error), the page controller sets an error state that can be used to display a generic error message and a retry control; retrying simply calls `getPrivacyPolicyContent` again.

Clicking an item in the table of contents updates the `section` URL parameter to the corresponding section `id`, scrolls to the matching section element, and updates the active section styling in both TOC and content regions.

Changing the `section` URL parameter via browser back/forward navigation triggers a handler that re-evaluates which section should be focused, ensuring the scrolled position and active states follow the URL.

Clicking a section’s direct-link control ensures that the current URL contains `?section={thatSectionId}` and may optionally copy this URL to the clipboard; this does not require any additional server calls beyond the initial `getPrivacyPolicyContent` request.

When no `section` parameter is present or when it does not match any loaded section `id`, the page shows the full policy without auto-scrolling, and no section is marked as active in the table of contents.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from the result of `getPrivacyPolicyContent` and the `section` URL parameter. On initial load, the page parses the query string for `section`. It then calls `getPrivacyPolicyContent` once; the returned `last_updated` and `sections` are stored in in-memory state managed by the page controller. After loading, if a `section` parameter is present and matches any `sections[i].id`, the focus manager scrolls to that section and marks it active; otherwise, the policy is simply displayed from the top. User interactions that change which section is active (e.g., clicks in the table of contents or section direct-link controls) update the `section` parameter in the URL using navigation methods that preserve browser history. The page listens for URL changes (including browser back/forward) and re-runs the section-focus logic against the already-loaded `sections` without recalling `getPrivacyPolicyContent`. All state transitions (loading, loaded, error, active section) are handled in memory and by URL parameters only; no localStorage or other persistent client storage mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of the privacy policy data, manages page-level states (loading, loaded, error), reads and reacts to the `section` URL parameter, and passes the loaded data to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse the URL query string for `section`, then invoke `getPrivacyPolicyContent`. Store the returned `last_updated` and `sections` in controller state and notify child components that data is ready.

onUrlChange: Listen for changes to the URL query string (e.g., browser back/forward). If the `section` parameter changes, emit a section-change event with the new `section` value so the focus manager and TOC can update; do not recall `getPrivacyPolicyContent` unless there is no existing data.
onRetryLoad: When a retry control is activated after an error, clear the error state and call `getPrivacyPolicyContent` again, then reinitialize child components with the new result.

components.functionality: Displays the static page title (e.g., 'Privacy Policy') and the dynamic last-updated information returned by `getPrivacyPolicyContent`.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataReady: When the page controller provides the result of `getPrivacyPolicyContent`, bind and render the `last_updated` value in the header.

components.functionality: Renders a navigable table of contents based on the `sections` array returned by `getPrivacyPolicyContent`, highlights the currently active section, and updates the URL when a section is selected.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataReady: Receive the `sections` array from the page controller and build a list of TOC items, each associated with `sections[i].id` and `sections[i].title`.

onTocItemClick(sectionId): Prevent default navigation, update the browser URL to include `?section={sectionId}` (preserving other query parameters if needed), and emit a section-change event so the focus manager can scroll to the selected section.

onActiveSectionChange(sectionId): When notified by the page controller or focus manager that the active section has changed (e.g., due to URL change), update the TOC UI to mark the matching section as active.

components.functionality: Renders all sections of the Privacy Policy returned by `getPrivacyPolicyContent`. Each section is output with a DOM element whose ID is bound to `sections[i].id` and whose content uses `sections[i].title` and `sections[i].body_html`.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataReady: Receive the `sections` array and for each item create a section container with `id` equal to `sections[i].id`, displaying its `title` and injecting `body_html` safely into the DOM.

onActiveSectionChange(sectionId): When a section becomes active (based on `section` URL parameter changes), update styling for the corresponding section container (e.g., add an active or highlighted state).

components.functionality: Controls which section is scrolled into view and considered active based on the `section` URL parameter and user interactions from the TOC or direct-link actions.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataReady: After the sections are rendered, read the current `section` URL parameter. If it matches a section `id`, scroll that section into view and notify other components (such as TOC and content list) of the active section.

onUrlSectionChange(newSectionId): Triggered when the page controller detects a change to the `section` URL parameter. If `newSectionId` corresponds to a rendered section, scroll that section into view and broadcast an active-section update; if it does not match any section, clear any active-section highlight.

onManualSectionSelect(sectionId): When a section is chosen via a TOC click or direct-link control, scroll to the section and notify other components; this is often called after the URL update but can also be used for internal synchronization.

components.functionality: Provides per-section controls that ensure the current URL reflects the selected section via the `section` parameter and optionally expose that URL for copying/sharing.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataReady: For each section rendered in `privacy-section-content-list`, attach a direct-link control associated with that section’s `id`.

onDirectLinkClick(sectionId): Update the current URL to include `?section={sectionId}` (merging with any existing query parameters), then emit an active-section-change event to keep the focus manager and TOC synchronized; optionally copy the updated URL to the clipboard using browser capabilities.
onUrlSectionChange(sectionId): When the `section` URL parameter changes, update any direct-link control states (e.g., indicate which section’s link matches the current URL).

components.functionality: Manages and displays loading and error states related to the `getPrivacyPolicyContent` call, without hardcoding any content beyond generic status messaging hooks.
event_handlers: onLoadStart: When the page controller initiates `getPrivacyPolicyContent`, show a loading indicator state.
onLoadSuccess: When `getPrivacyPolicyContent` resolves successfully, hide the loading indicator and ensure any previous errors are cleared.
onLoadError: When `getPrivacyPolicyContent` fails, show an error state and expose a retry control that calls the controller’s `onRetryLoad` handler.
onRetryClick: When the user chooses to retry, delegate to the page controller’s `onRetryLoad` to attempt `getPrivacyPolicyContent` again.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (likely around 1140–1200px) and large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same top-to-bottom, centered flow as the mockup: a full-width hero with a darkened photographic background and centrally aligned privacy header, followed by a white background content band. Inside that band, content sits in a centered max-width container. The first row in this band places the table of contents and direct-link controls together in a left column card, with the detailed privacy sections in a wider right column. Invisible, behavioral components (focus manager, controller, feedback state) are layered logically around these visible elements but do not disrupt the linear visual stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same top-to-bottom, centered flow as the mockup: a full-width hero with a darkened photographic background and centrally aligned privacy header, followed by a white background content band. Inside that band, content sits in a centered max-width container. The first row in this band places the table of contents and direct-link controls together in a left column card, with the detailed privacy sections in a wider right column. Invisible, behavioral components (focus manager, controller, feedback state) are layered logically around these visible elements but do not disrupt the linear visual stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero (privacy-policy-header) occupies roughly the top 45–55% of the viewport height, with generous vertical breathing room around the title and summary. Below, the main white content area has large top and bottom padding (around 64–96px). Within this area, the left column (TOC + direct link controls) takes about 25–30% of the content width, and the right column (section content) takes about 70–75%. Vertical spacing between sections and major structural elements follows the 8px-based scale, with 32–64px gaps defining hierarchy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Despite the text-heavy nature of a privacy policy, the layout maintains a low-to-medium density: readable line lengths, generous paragraph spacing, and substantial padding inside cards. The hero has sparse, focused copy (title, short description, effective date). The TOC groups links with comfortable 12–16px vertical gaps, while section content uses 24–32px spacing between headings and body text to avoid visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the global nav (outside this scope) down into the floating feeling hero where the 'Privacy Policy' header is the first focal point, centered over the dark image. From there, the eye drops into the white content band, first catching the orange-accent TOC card on the left, then sliding across to the initial section heading in the main content on the right (a soft Z-pattern). As users scroll, the section-focus-manager and TOC highlight the active section, while the direct-link controls stay visually associated with the TOC. The feedback state appears just above the main content row, briefly drawing attention when there is a loading or error state, then receding when inactive.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page mirrors the marketing hero + content pattern from the mockup but adapts it for a documentation-like experience. A darkened, full-width background photo (e.g., a moving truck at dusk) fills the top half of the screen, with a softly elevated, centered content column that holds the privacy-policy-header: a bold, orange-accent page title, a short one-line explanation of the policy, and an effective date, all vertically and horizontally centered and surrounded by ample whitespace. Scrolling past the hero transitions to a clean white background band where the main policy content lives inside a centered max-width container. This container is split into two columns on larger screens: on the left, a tall, rounded, orange TOC card with a slight shadow lists all policy sections and embeds the direct-link controls; on the right, a wide, white content column stacks each policy section with clear headings and well-spaced paragraphs. A subtle feedback-status card appears between the hero and this content area when the policy is loading or encounters an error. Invisible behavioral components ensure that as users click or scroll to specific sections, the corresponding TOC item is highlighted and deep links update smoothly, all while preserving the calm, spacious, corporate-friendly aesthetic of the original design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the full-page structural wrapper. It stretches from the very top of the viewport to the bottom, spanning 100% of the browser width. Inside, it defines a centered max-width content container of roughly 1140–1200px that all visible components (hero, TOC, sections, feedback) align to. The controller enforces the vertical stacking order: hero (privacy-policy-header) at the top, followed by a narrow horizontal feedback area, then the main content band containing the TOC and section content. It also establishes the 12-column responsive grid, with generous left and right gutters so that even on large screens the main content sits comfortably centered with ample outer whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-page container; it scrolls with the page and primarily governs layout constraints, breakpoints, and inter-component spacing rather than rendering visible UI of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy header occupies a full-width hero band directly below the global navigation. It uses a dark, desaturated photographic background related to moving services, with a slight overlay to increase contrast. The hero height is approximately 45–55% of the viewport on desktop, shrinking proportionally on smaller screens but never feeling cramped. Within this band, the content is centered both horizontally and vertically inside the main max-width container. The title 'Privacy Policy' sits in a large, bold typeface in a bright orange or white color, with a short single-line description and an 'Effective date' line stacked beneath it, each separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. All text is center-aligned, and the content block occupies roughly 50–60% of the container width, leaving generous space to the left and right. There is ample padding above and below the text (48–64px), preserving the airy, hero-like feeling. The hero visually anchors the top of the page and clearly separates itself from the white background section that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero section: it scrolls off the top as the user moves down. The background image is set to cover and remain fixed or slowly parallaxed (optional) to preserve a premium, floating impression similar to the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents appears in the main white content band immediately below the hero and any visible feedback state. Within the centered max-width container, the TOC occupies a left column that is approximately 3–4 of the 12 grid columns (about 25–30% of the content width). It is rendered as a tall, vertically oriented card with a bright orange background, large rounded corners, and a soft drop shadow, echoing the wide orange card seen in the mockup. The card begins roughly 64–80px below the bottom edge of the hero, with matching outer margin to the right where the section content begins. Inside the card, a compact heading such as 'On this page' sits at the top with 24–32px padding from the edges. Below, section links are listed in a vertical stack, each line separated by 12–16px of vertical space, with 16–24px of padding on left and right. The text is white or very light to contrast with the orange background, and current/active section links receive a stronger emphasis (e.g., bold or with a small green indicator bar on the left) to guide reading. The overall TOC card height extends down to roughly the mid-height of the first several sections, visually anchoring the left side of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the TOC card behaves as a sticky element within its column: once scrolled into view, it remains pinned near the top of the viewport (with a top offset of about 80–96px to avoid overlapping any fixed header) while the main section content scrolls on the right. On smaller screens, it drops into a full-width card above the section content, losing the sticky behavior and becoming a horizontally scrollable or tap-to-expand list if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section content list occupies the primary reading column to the right of the TOC. Inside the same centered max-width container, it spans the remaining 8–9 grid columns (about 70–75% of the content width) on desktop, beginning at the same vertical height as the top of the TOC card. The column maintains generous left padding (24–32px) where it meets the TOC to prevent visual crowding. Each policy section is presented as part of a continuous, vertical stack: a section heading in a medium-large font, followed by one or more paragraphs with comfortable 24px line spacing. There is 32–48px of vertical space between the end of one section and the heading of the next, using the 8px spacing system. Important subpoints may be presented as indented lists with 16px vertical gaps. The content background is white with a subtle differentiation—either a very light border or slight shadow around each section’s block—to mirror the soft-card feeling of the mockup without overwhelming a text-heavy page. The entire column maintains a readable line length (approximately 60–80 characters), and margins at the top and bottom (64–96px) give the section content room to breathe within the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling behavior: this column scrolls vertically as users move through the policy. Each individual section is anchorable (with unique IDs) so that clicking a TOC item scrolls smoothly to the corresponding section, aligning the section heading slightly below the top of the viewport to account for any sticky elements. The list coexists with the sticky TOC on wide screens and spans full width below the TOC on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section focus manager is not visually rendered as a distinct UI element but is conceptually aligned with the section content column. It spans the vertical height of the main content area, monitoring the scroll position relative to each section within the privacy-section-content-list. Its effective area corresponds to the full width and height of the right content column, roughly 70–75% of the container width within the white content band, and tracks which section heading is closest to the top of the viewport. This logical overlay ensures that whenever a section becomes the primary focus in the reading flow, the corresponding item in the TOC card is highlighted and any direct-link controls update accordingly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an invisible, scroll-aware layer that moves with the page. It does not occupy space or affect layout; instead, it hooks into the scroll behavior of the main content area to manage focus, deep-link state, and ARIA-related focus adjustments for accessibility when users navigate via TOC links or URL parameters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The direct-link controls are visually integrated into the same left column as the TOC to maintain a clear navigational cluster. Within the orange TOC card, they appear as a compact control strip near the bottom of the card, separated from the main list of section links by a subtle divider line or 16–24px of extra vertical space. The controls are arranged in a horizontal row of small pill-shaped buttons or icon-label chips (e.g., 'Copy link', 'View section only'), each with 8–12px of padding and slight inner spacing of 8px between them. These pills use a lighter shade of orange or white background with orange/green text to ensure sufficient contrast against the main card body. The control strip aligns flush with the left padding of the TOC content and occupies roughly the bottom 20–25% of the card’s height, but its actual height flexes based on the number of controls. This positioning keeps deep-link actions visually associated with section navigation without competing with the main reading area on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the direct-link controls move together with the sticky TOC card, staying visible while scrolling through sections. On small screens, they follow the TOC into a full-width card above the content and stack either horizontally (if space allows) or wrap to multiple lines; they scroll normally with the content and do not remain fixed independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback state appears as a slim, horizontally oriented status card in the white band that begins the main content area. It sits directly below the hero (privacy-policy-header) and above the row that contains the TOC and section content, aligned to the same centered max-width container. The card spans approximately 80–100% of the container width but remains inset from the browser edges by the same generous side gutters as other content. It has softly rounded corners and 12–16px of padding on all sides, using background colors that align with status (e.g., soft green for loaded, orange for warning, red for errors) and subtle iconography on the left, followed by concise text on the right. The vertical spacing above and below this feedback card is around 24–32px, so that when it is visible it feels integrated but does not crowd either the hero above or the TOC/content row below. When there is no active status, the card collapses completely so that the TOC and section content move up seamlessly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically, pushing content below it down when present. It scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed; users encounter it as a transient state just beneath the hero when loading or error conditions occur.
